summary: Audit build-v3 Postgres cybersecurity across 6 dimensions, adversarially verify, research fixes (incl. data-cleaning), red-team critique
logs: Audit+verify done: 51 findings, 50 survived, 50 after dedup

confirmed.title: runtime.* has NO RLS and api holds full INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on session/otp/password_reset_token — one app bug = platform-wide account takeover (blast radius wider than auth)
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:26,29 (grant + default DML on all runtime to api) + docs/migration/build-v3/30_runtime.sql:12-202 (no `enable row level security` anywhere in runtime)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced in umi_backfill_v3: `set role api; insert into runtime.password_reset_token(user_id,token_hash,expires_at) select id,'attacker-known-hash',now()+'1 day' from umi.user limit 1` → INSERT 0 1 with NO app.current_business set (runtime has zero policies). `update runtime.session set user_id=...` also permitted. Any IDOR/SQLi reaching the api role mints a reset token or forges/rewrites a session for ANY user on ANY tenant incl platform superadmin → complete cross-tenant account takeover. I additionally confirmed api has ins/upd/del on ALL 16 runtime tables: I injected a runtime.outbox_event('whatsapp.send', phishing payload) that the worker would deliver (arbitrary outbound message as any café), and integration_sync/conversation_state/reminder_sent/pass_device are all cross-tenant writable — so the blast radius is broader than auth-token forgery: outbound-message injection, sync-cursor poisoning, and bot-state tampering across every tenant.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove api's write access to auth + machinery: runtime.session/otp/password_reset_token/device_session/pairing (and ideally outbox_event/integration_sync) should be reachable only through the worker (BYPASSRLS) pool or SECURITY DEFINER auth functions that validate the acting principal. If api must self-service its own session, enable RLS keyed to a per-request app.user_id GUC rather than blanket DML. No runtime table should be writable by a request-path role while it carries no RLS.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Empirically reproduced the exact INSERT the auditor described (INSERT 0 1, no GUC). pg_class shows runtime = 16 tables, 0 relrowsecurity. Went beyond: proved api can write ALL 16 runtime tables cross-tenant and injected an outbox_event, which escalates impact from account-takeover to arbitrary outbound messaging + sync poisoning. Critical is correct; if anything the auditor under-scoped the blast radius.

confirmed.title: umi.* has NO RLS and api/readonly can SELECT umi.user.password_hash plus the entire cross-tenant identity/authz/billing graph
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:15,24 (grant select on all umi to api + readonly) + docs/migration/build-v3/10_umi.sql:27-42 (password_hash/password_algorithm), no `enable row level security` in 10_umi.sql
confirmed.failure_scenario: Confirmed: `set role api; select count(password_hash) from umi.user` → 8; same for readonly. Distribution is scrypt-sha256-v1 x6, legacy-sha256-v1 x2, null x1 — the legacy-sha256 archival hashes are trivially crackable. umi has 0 RLS policies (verified 0/16 relrowsecurity), so one `select *`/logging bug or injection on umi.user leaks crackable credentials for the whole platform. api also holds SELECT on umi.user_role (full cross-tenant authorization grant graph), umi.subscription, umi.invoice, umi.prospect and umi.audit_log (all confirmed has_table_privilege = true) → a café-facing role can read every other café's plan, billing amounts, sales pipeline and platform audit trail.
confirmed.recommendation: Revoke SELECT on umi.user from api and readonly; expose only non-secret columns (id,email,full_name,status,last_login_at) via a column-level grant or view, and verify passwords inside a SECURITY DEFINER function (or worker pool) so the hash never enters the request-path role. Limit api's umi reads to genuinely-global catalogs (role, permission, role_permission, channel_type, feature, plan, plan_feature); put RLS or definer-function access in front of user_role/subscription/invoice/prospect/audit_log.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced hash disclosure to both api and readonly and confirmed the cross-tenant graph is fully readable by api. The weak legacy hashes plus zero DB containment make credential compromise realistic from a single request-path bug. Critical is correct.

confirmed.title: tenant.conversation_analytics view leaks cross-tenant rows — not security_invoker, so RLS is evaluated as the view owner and bypassed
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/20_tenant.sql:502-510 (create view, no security_invoker) + 90_rls.sql:25 (grant DML on ALL TABLES includes the view)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced in umi_backfill_v3: `set role api; set app.current_business='<El Gran Ribera>'` (0 conversations). `select count(*) from tenant.conversation` → 0 (base RLS works), but `select count(*) from tenant.conversation_analytics` → 11, all belonging to Kalala (business_id 1860305f...). pg_class.reloptions on the view is NULL → security_invoker=false → the view runs with owner rights, and the owner is exempt from the underlying tables' (non-forced) RLS, so isolation is bypassed inside the view. In prod the migration runner (postgres/supabase_admin) owns both view and tables, so the bypass holds. Any café calling an endpoint backed by this granted view reads every other café's conversation analytics with no second bug required.
confirmed.recommendation: Create the view WITH (security_invoker=true) so underlying-table RLS evaluates as the calling api role. Grant only SELECT on views (the blanket `on all tables` also handed api INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on it — confirmed). umi.effective_entitlement is the same pattern (returns all tenants) but moot while umi has no RLS.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Directly reproduced the cross-tenant leak (0 base rows vs 11 view rows from a different business). This is the most directly-exploitable disclosure of the set (no injection needed, just the grant + query). Only two views exist (conversation_analytics, effective_entitlement); both lack reloptions. Data sensitivity (conversation counts/durations) is lower than credentials, so high (not critical) is the right rating.

confirmed.title: tenant tables enable RLS but never FORCE it — the table owner bypasses all isolation and enables the view leak
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:39-129 (enable, no `force row level security`); confirmed relforcerowsecurity=false on all 33 tenant tables
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: tenant = 33 tables, 33 relrowsecurity=true, 0 relforcerowsecurity. With FORCE off, the table owner is exempt from every tenant_isolation policy. This is the root cause that makes the conversation_analytics leak possible, and it means isolation silently evaporates the moment any connection runs as the owner: a migration/admin connection reused for a query, a DATABASE_URL that points the request path at the owner/BYPASSRLS role, or a future decision to make api own the tables. In-DB isolation currently depends entirely on the process happening to connect as a non-owner, non-BYPASSRLS role — an operational assumption with no enforcement.
confirmed.recommendation: Add `alter table tenant.<t> force row level security;` for every tenant base table (extend the do-block in 90_rls.sql). Combined with security_invoker views this closes the owner-bypass path and makes the leak test return zero rows even when the caller is the table owner.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: relforcerowsecurity=false confirmed on all 33 tables. On its own the request-path api role is a non-owner in prod so normal RLS holds — so part of this is defense-in-depth — but it is the direct enabler of the confirmed F3 view leak and it removes an entire class of owner-bypass/mis-config risk for a one-line-per-table fix. High is justified given F3 depends on it and the demonstrated role-configuration drift (F5/F10).

confirmed.title: RLS GUC name drift: build-v3 policies read app.current_business, but the backend sets app.tenant_id — a cutover landmine on the isolation linchpin
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced: `set role api; select set_config('app.tenant_id','<Kalala>',false); select count(*) from tenant.customer where business_id='<Kalala>'` → 0 rows, because the policy reads app.current_business (unset → NULL → 0). Setting app.current_business instead → 73 rows. So at cutover the backend (which sets app.tenant_id, correctly transaction-scoped via set_config(...,true), server-derived from AuthGuard) would make every RLS query return zero rows and break the whole app — exactly the pressure that leads to a rushed policy loosening or a switch to the BYPASSRLS worker pool. No test asserts the setter name equals the policy name. Note: the default PgService.query() path already runs on the BYPASSRLS worker pool with manual tenant_id predicates, so RLS is not even in force for most repository calls today — a pre-existing leak surface if any query omits its WHERE.
confirmed.recommendation: Pick one GUC name and enforce it in both artifacts (rename policies to app.tenant_id, or set app.current_business in pg.service.ts) and add a startup/CI assertion that the name the code sets equals the name the policies read. Audit which paths use withTenant() (app pool, RLS) vs query()/workerTx() (worker pool, BYPASSRLS + manual predicate) and move tenant-scoped reads onto the RLS pool.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Confirmed in code (pg.service.ts sets app.tenant_id/app.user_id) and empirically (app.tenant_id yields 0 rows; only app.current_business scopes). This is primarily an availability/correctness landmine whose security angle is the loosening it invites; the confirmed pre-existing worker-pool-BYPASSRLS default path makes the underlying concern real. High is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Over-broad blanket grants: api gets DML on views, readonly gets SELECT on password hashes and runtime auth tokens
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:15 (readonly select all), 19 (worker), 24-29 (api select-all umi + DML-all tenant/runtime)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Confirmed: `grant select,insert,update,delete on all tables in schema tenant to api` also targeted the tenant.conversation_analytics VIEW (api has ins/upd on it = true). `grant select on all tables ... to readonly` gave the reporting role SELECT on umi.user.password_hash (read 8 hashes), runtime.otp and runtime.password_reset_token (has_table_privilege = true). A leaked read-only analytics credential thus exposes all platform credentials and live auth tokens, not just business metrics. Grants are coarse schema-wide sweeps with no carve-out for secret columns/tables, and the alter-default-privileges lines will auto-grant future secret tables the same way.
confirmed.recommendation: Replace schema-wide grants with per-table grants that exclude the secret surface: readonly should not see umi.user secret columns or any runtime auth table; api should get SELECT-only (not DML) on views and no write on runtime auth tables. Scope `alter default privileges` narrowly so future secret tables aren't auto-granted.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced readonly reading 8 password hashes + runtime auth tables, and api holding DML on the view. This is the least-privilege root cause behind F1/F2/F3; as a standalone grant-hygiene finding medium is the right rating (the credential-exposure impact is already scored critical under F1/F2).

confirmed.title: Empty-string app.current_business hard-errors every RLS query instead of failing closed gracefully
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:41-129 (all policies do `= current_setting('app.current_business',true)::uuid` with no nullif/empty guard)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Confirmed: with app.current_business='' , `select count(*) from tenant.conversation` → ERROR: invalid input syntax for type uuid: "". A truly never-set GUC fails closed cleanly (current_setting(...,true) IS NULL → 0 rows — I verified this in a fresh session), and RESET yields '' which also errors. So an empty-string GUC makes the policy expression itself throw and every query on any RLS table aborts. The backend already uses this exact coercion pattern (pg.service.ts:96 sets app.user_id to `userId ?? ''`), so any path that derives the business GUC from a null tenant id as '' would take the whole request path down (self-inflicted DoS) rather than degrade safely.
confirmed.recommendation: Guard the cast in every policy: `= nullif(current_setting('app.current_business', true),'')::uuid`, so both unset and empty-string resolve to NULL (fail-closed, zero rows). Alternatively normalize the GUC to NULL when blank before setting it.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced the empty-string error and separately confirmed the auditor's fail-closed claim for the truly-never-set case (IS NULL → 0 rows), and clarified that RESET produces '' (also errors). Impact is a latent self-DoS gated on a '' being set; the `?? ''` pattern already in the codebase makes it a plausible foot-gun and the nullif fix also hardens fail-closed posture. Medium is fair (arguably medium-low).

confirmed.title: FK checks bypass RLS → cross-tenant existence oracle and reference contamination on tenant child rows
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/20_tenant.sql:399 (customer_order.customer_id FK) and generally every tenant FK to a sibling tenant table
confirmed.failure_scenario: Reproduced in umi_backfill_v3: as api scoped to Kalala, `update tenant.customer_order set customer_id='<a Northwest-Café customer uuid>' where id='<a Kalala order>'` → UPDATE 1 (accepted; referential-integrity check runs with RLS bypassed), while the same update with a non-existent uuid → FK violation. So a café can (a) probe whether any UUID is a live customer/order/card anywhere on the platform via accept-vs-FK-error, and (b) attach its own rows to another tenant's entities, creating dangling cross-tenant references. It cannot read the foreign rows back (base-table RLS still blocks SELECT), so impact is limited to enumeration + referential pollution.
confirmed.recommendation: Accept as a known Postgres RLS caveat but reduce blast radius: validate FK targets on user-supplied columns resolve within the current business at the app layer before write, avoid returning FK-constraint error text to clients, and where feasible use composite (business_id, id) FKs so a foreign target fails the tenant-scoped reference rather than succeeding.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced both arms (real foreign customer accepted, bogus uuid rejected). EGR had no orders so I used Kalala as the acting business and a Northwest-Café customer as the foreign target — same mechanism the auditor described. Impact genuinely capped at enumeration/pollution since SELECT-back is blocked; low is correct.

confirmed.title: Verified safe: tenant RLS coverage is complete and parent-scoped child writes are correctly blocked
confirmed.failure_scenario: No failure. Verified: all 33 tenant base tables have RLS enabled AND exactly one tenant_isolation policy (query for tables with policy count <>1 returned empty). The child policies are FOR ALL with only a USING parent-exists clause and no explicit WITH CHECK, but that is not a write gap: for FOR ALL policies Postgres reuses USING as the WITH CHECK on INSERT/UPDATE. Reproduced: as api scoped to El Gran Ribera, `insert into tenant.order_item(...) values('<a Kalala order>', ...)` → 'new row violates row-level security policy for table order_item'. So a café cannot write into another café's child rows via these policies.
confirmed.recommendation: No change required for correctness; optionally add explicit WITH CHECK mirroring the USING clause on the child policies for readability and to stay robust if any policy is later split from FOR ALL into per-command policies (where the USING→WITH CHECK reuse would no longer apply).
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Independently reproduced the blocked child write and confirmed complete policy coverage (33/33, exactly one policy each). The auditor's safe-verification is accurate; recorded as confirmed = the safety claim holds. Note it does NOT contradict F3/F4: the owner-bypass leak is a separate axis from these non-owner child-write checks.

confirmed.title: Backend connects as umi_app/umi_worker but build-v3 only configures api/worker/readonly — cutover role reconciliation is unaddressed and can silently disable RLS
confirmed.location: prod-config: apps/umi-api/src/shared/database/pg.service.ts:15-16,52 (umi_app/umi_worker) + DATABASE_URL_APP/DATABASE_URL_WORKER vs docs/migration/build-v3/00_foundation.sql:10-15 (creates api/worker/readonly) + 90_rls.sql:12-29 (grants only to api/worker/readonly)
confirmed.failure_scenario: build-v3 grants privileges only to api/worker/readonly (verified grant counts: api 215, worker 264, readonly 67); the leftover umi_app/umi_worker roles receive zero build-v3 grants. The umi-api backend still names umi_app/umi_worker (comments, the onModuleInit log, and the request-context doc) and reads DATABASE_URL_APP/DATABASE_URL_WORKER. At cutover, if DATABASE_URL_APP still points at umi_app the request path gets permission-denied everywhere; if it points at umi_worker (which exists with BYPASSRLS) the request path runs with RLS fully bypassed — a silent, total tenant-isolation failure with no error. Nothing in build-v3 or a test asserts DATABASE_URL_APP resolves to the RLS-enforced api role.
confirmed.recommendation: Reconcile role names before cutover: repoint DATABASE_URL_APP → api (RLS-enforced, non-BYPASSRLS) and DATABASE_URL_WORKER → worker, update the pg.service.ts comments/log, and add a startup assertion that the app pool's current_user is non-superuser AND NOT pg_has_role(current_user,'...','bypassrls') so a connection that would bypass RLS fails fast instead of silently leaking. Pair this with the F5 GUC rename so both halves of the RLS linchpin are verified in one gate.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Confirmed the artifact divergence empirically: build-v3 configures api/worker/readonly and the leftover umi_app/umi_worker have no build-v3 grants, while the backend code names umi_app/umi_worker. Marked plausible (not confirmed) because the decisive fact — which role DATABASE_URL_APP actually resolves to at cutover — lives in unreadable prod env config; if it is repointed to api this is a no-op, but if it lands on the BYPASSRLS role RLS is silently off. High given the isolation-defeating downside and that it compounds F5.

confirmed.title: Request-path role `api` can forge sessions and mint auth for ANY user (full auth bypass / cross-tenant privilege escalation)
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:26 (grant DML on all runtime.* to api) + 30_runtime.sql:18-53
confirmed.failure_scenario: Re-verified on umi_build_v3 AND umi_backfill_v3: has_table_privilege('api','runtime.session','INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE')=t, ('api','runtime.otp','INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE')=t, ('api','runtime.device_session','INSERT')=t, ('api','runtime.password_reset_token','UPDATE')=t, and has_column_privilege('api','runtime.session','token_hash','SELECT')=t. `api` is the role EVERY café request runs as. A SQLi or session-minting logic bug in the request path can `INSERT INTO runtime.session(user_id, app, token_hash, expires_at) VALUES (<any umi.user id>, 'dashboard', <hash of attacker-known token>, now()+'1y')` then present the matching token to authenticate as that user, or UPDATE runtime.session.revoked_at/expires_at to un-revoke/extend a stolen session, or reset runtime.otp.consumed_at to replay a one-time code — all with zero DB backstop. Impact is genuinely cross-tenant: backfill data shows one user (hola@umiconsulting.co) holds admin on 4 businesses (users_with_multi_business=1, distinct_businesses_in_grants=5), so impersonating that identity reaches 4 tenants at once.
confirmed.recommendation: Move all session/otp/password_reset writes out of the request path. Either (a) a dedicated `auth` role invoked via a narrow SECURITY DEFINER function that stamps user_id from the verified principal (never caller input), or (b) at minimum grant `api` INSERT-only where it truly mints its own sessions and REVOKE INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on runtime.session, runtime.otp, runtime.device_session, runtime.pairing, runtime.password_reset_token from `api`.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Every privilege claim reproduced on both live DBs. One caveat on the exploit narrative: bare IDOR (accessing another object through the app's own parameterized queries) does NOT by itself grant an arbitrary INSERT into runtime.session — that path realistically needs SQLi or a session-minting logic flaw. But the defense-in-depth thesis is the whole point of a pre-cutover hardening pass: the request-path role must not own the auth-material lifecycle, and here it holds full DML over it. Cross-tenant escalation is real (verified multi-tenant admin user). Critical is warranted; this is the sharpest finding in the set and #4 is a subset of it.

confirmed.title: `api` has blanket SELECT on ALL of schema umi — every tenant's password hashes, RBAC map, financials and sealed platform audit are readable by the café request role
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:24 (`grant select on all tables in schema umi to api`)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Confirmed: has_column_privilege('api','umi.user','password_hash','SELECT')=t and umi has NO RLS (0 of 16 umi tables have relrowsecurity=t on both DBs), so the grant is unfiltered across all tenants. api also has SELECT on umi.user_role (12 grants / 5 businesses — the full cross-tenant RBAC map), umi.subscription (5 rows), umi.invoice, umi.prospect and umi.audit_log (has_table_privilege api SELECT = t for each). One request-path read bug/injection → offline cracking material for every operator credential + reconnaissance of who-can-do-what in every tenant + Umi's own billing.
confirmed.recommendation: Drop the blanket `grant select … on umi to api`. Grant api SELECT only on the catalog/entitlement tables it needs (umi.role, umi.permission, umi.role_permission, umi.channel_type, umi.feature, umi.plan, umi.plan_feature, and the umi.effective_entitlement view), and for login use column-scoped `grant select (id, email, status, password_hash, password_algorithm) on umi.user` — not full-table. Deny api on umi.invoice, umi.subscription(_item), umi.prospect(_event), umi.user_role, umi.audit_log, umi.entitlement_override.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Structurally confirmed and the over-grant is real. I down-rate critical→high: (1) exposed material is HASHED, and per project context it is archival scrypt-sha256 (x6, expensive to crack) + legacy-sha256 (x2) + 1 null — not plaintext; (2) umi.audit_log and umi.prospect are EMPTY in the backfill dataset (umi_audit_rows=0, prospect_rows=0), so the 'sealed audit trail' / 'sales-pipeline PII' read-exposure is structural/future, not present in the current data; (3) exploitation still needs an app-layer read bug. The auditor's specific data claims of '5 subscription rows' and '12 grants/5 businesses' are correct, but the '5 audit rows' and prospect-PII specifics are not present today. Still a genuine least-privilege violation on the highest-blast-radius role; high, bordering critical only for the password_hash column.

confirmed.title: RBAC scope (business_id/branch_id) and the permission model are STORED but never enforced in the database — RLS trusts app.current_business blindly
confirmed.failure_scenario: Grep of the whole build-v3 tree returns ZERO references to umi.user_role, umi.role_permission, umi.permission, umi.has_permission, or an app.current_user GUC inside any policy or function. Every tenant policy is solely `business_id = current_setting('app.current_business', true)::uuid`. The DB verifies only that SOME business id was set — never that the authenticated principal holds a user_role grant for it, and never checks any permission key. And the model is not merely unenforced, it is unwired: role_permission has 0 rows (4 permission keys defined, none mapped). So if app code sets app.current_business to a business the user isn't entitled to (tenant-id-from-request bug, or a stale value on a pooled connection), RLS happily serves/mutates that tenant.
confirmed.recommendation: Bind the session to the principal in-DB: add app.current_user alongside app.current_business and extend tenant policies to also require `exists (select 1 from umi.user_role ur where ur.user_id = current_setting('app.current_user',true)::uuid and (ur.business_id = current_setting('app.current_business',true)::uuid or ur.business_id is null))`. Enforce privileged-action permission keys via a SECURITY DEFINER umi.has_permission(user,business,key) in the writing functions. Until then, document explicitly that tenant isolation is app-enforced only and the scope/permission columns are decorative.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Grep and the empty role_permission table confirm the model is inert. Partly this is a deliberate architecture (business-scoped RLS + app-owned principal→business binding), so it's not a live exploit today — hence not critical — but it is a real single-point-of-failure: all authorization rests on one app-set GUC with no DB backstop, and the stored scope columns give a false impression of enforcement. High is appropriate for a pre-cutover hardening pass; the empty role_permission finding strengthens the auditor's case.

confirmed.title: `api` has full UPDATE/DELETE on all runtime auth tables (otp, password_reset_token, session) — request path can tamper with the auth substrate
confirmed.failure_scenario: Confirmed: has_table_privilege('api','runtime.otp','UPDATE'/'DELETE')=t, ('api','runtime.password_reset_token','UPDATE')=t, ('api','runtime.session','DELETE')=t. A request-path bug can reset otp.consumed_at/expires_at to replay/extend a one-time code, flip password_reset_token.used_at to reuse a consumed reset, or DELETE session rows to erase the auth trail — none of which is a legitimate need of a per-request café role.
confirmed.recommendation: Scope api on runtime to only the machinery it drives (conversation_state, outbox_event insert, idempotency_key, reminder_sent) and strip UPDATE/DELETE on the auth-material tables. Fold this into the same revoke as finding #1 and fix the ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES line (finding #6) so future runtime tables aren't auto-armed.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: All privilege checks reproduced. This is real but is essentially the same root over-grant as finding #1 (identical grant line 90_rls.sql:26, same tables) sliced as tamper-vs-forge; it should be MERGED with #1 rather than tracked as an independent issue, and its fix is the same single revoke. High as a standalone facet is fine; note the overlap so it isn't double-counted as separate remediation work.

confirmed.title: RLS is ENABLED but not FORCED on any tenant table — isolation silently voids if the backend connects as the table owner or a superuser
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: all 33 tenant tables have relrowsecurity=t but relforcerowsecurity=f; api/worker/readonly are all NOLOGIN (rolcanlogin=f) so prod MUST add a separate login role; and every tenant table is owned by juanlopez1 (a superuser) here — on Supabase the migration is applied as `postgres`, which then owns every table. RLS never applies to a table's owner. If the VPS backend's connection string uses that owner role (or any superuser, or a login role that is a MEMBER of the owning role), RLS is bypassed entirely and app.current_business is ignored — total cross-tenant exposure while isolation appears 'on'.
confirmed.recommendation: Add `alter table tenant.<t> force row level security` for every tenant base table (same dynamic loop as the enable step). Ensure the prod login role is a plain, non-owner, non-superuser, non-BYPASSRLS member of `api`, and that tables are owned by a role the app never authenticates as. Assert relforcerowsecurity and `not rolbypassrls` on the login role in 99_verify.sql, and mandate SET LOCAL (not SET) with transaction-mode pooling.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: relforcerowsecurity=f verified on all 33 tables; no non-super login role exists yet; tables owned by a superuser. Important nuance the auditor slightly blurs: FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY matters specifically for the OWNER-connection case — if the backend correctly connects as a plain non-owner member of `api`, plain ENABLE already applies RLS and FORCE is belt-and-suspenders. On Supabase the realistic failure mode is exactly connecting as postgres/owner, so adding FORCE + asserting the login role's attributes is the right hardening. High is justified as a config/deployment gate that could otherwise nullify all tenant isolation.

confirmed.title: ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES auto-arms `api` and `worker` on every FUTURE table — least-privilege silently regresses as the schema grows
confirmed.failure_scenario: pg_default_acl confirms standing defaults exactly as claimed: future umi tables → {api=r, worker=arwd, readonly=r}; future tenant AND runtime tables → {api=arwd, worker=arwd, readonly=r}. Any table added later inherits broad grants with no conscious decision — a future umi.payment_method (card tokens) or new runtime auth table would be auto-readable/writable by the café request role the moment it is created.
confirmed.recommendation: Drop the `alter default privileges … to api` (and reconsider the worker ones); grant api table-by-table so each new table forces an explicit decision. Keep a default only for readonly SELECT if desired, established under the exact role that will own objects in prod.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: pg_default_acl output matches the finding precisely. One correction to the auditor's aside: the defaults ARE owner-scoped to whoever ran 90_rls (juanlopez1 here / postgres on Supabase); since that same role owns the objects in prod, the defaults will in fact cover prod-created tables — so the 'won't cover a different owner / inconsistent surface' worry is the weaker half. The core point (least-privilege silently regresses on future tables) is valid. Medium is right.

confirmed.title: `worker` (BYPASSRLS) holds full DML on umi.user_role and umi.user — any worker-context bug mints a platform-wide grant
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/00_foundation.sql:13 (worker BYPASSRLS) + 90_rls.sql:19 (worker full DML on all umi)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: worker rolbypassrls=t and has INSERT on umi.user_role, umi.user, umi.role, umi.role_permission, umi.plan. worker is the role that consumes untrusted external input (runtime.inbound_event holds raw Twilio/Zettle/Google-Wallet webhook envelopes). A parser/injection bug in a worker job can `INSERT INTO umi.user_role(user_id, role_id, business_id) VALUES (<attacker>, <any role>, NULL)` — a platform-wide grant — or read/rewrite any tenant. Money ledgers and audit_log are the exception: worker=ar (no UPDATE/DELETE) on audit_log verified.
confirmed.recommendation: Narrow worker on umi — it rarely needs to write umi.user, user_role, role, permission, role_permission, invoice, subscription. Give worker DML only on the runtime queue/integration tables and the tenant tables its jobs touch; keep BYPASSRLS but pair it with a tight table-grant list, and route identity/entitlement mutations through SECURITY DEFINER functions with server-side authorization.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: All grants and worker=ar-on-audit_log reproduced. One precision: there is currently NO is_platform role (platform_roles is empty; role catalog = admin/staff/owner/viewer), so 'self-granted superadmin' would today mean a broad admin grant, but worker CAN still write business_id=NULL (platform-wide) with any role, so the escalation shape holds. Requires a worker-context code-execution/injection bug (a higher bar than the request path), so medium — not high — is correct.

confirmed.title: `readonly` (reporting/diagnostics) can read all credentials and auth secrets across every tenant
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:15 (`grant select on all tables … to readonly`)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: has_column_privilege('readonly','umi.user','password_hash','SELECT')=t; has_table_privilege('readonly','runtime.otp','SELECT')=t; has_column_privilege('readonly','runtime.session','token_hash','SELECT')=t; plus umi.invoice/prospect SELECT=t. readonly is the credential handed to BI tools / ad-hoc diagnostics — the most likely to be shared or embedded — and has no business seeing password/OTP/reset hashes or live session token hashes. A leaked readonly DSN = wholesale credential + session-token exposure with no write needed.
confirmed.recommendation: After the blanket grant, `revoke select on umi.user, runtime.otp, runtime.password_reset_token, runtime.session, runtime.device_session, runtime.pairing from readonly` (better: grant readonly explicitly per non-sensitive table or via reporting views omitting secret columns). Drop readonly SELECT on umi.invoice/subscription/prospect unless a diagnostic case needs it.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: All privilege checks reproduced. Same blanket-grant pattern as #2 but on a role whose credential-leak likelihood is higher and whose write blast radius is nil, so medium is the right relative weight. Confirmed.

confirmed.title: Append-only audit rests on GRANT revokes only (no trigger); owner/superuser can still rewrite history, and trigger functions run as invoker
confirmed.failure_scenario: Confirmed: api/worker/readonly lack UPDATE/DELETE on umi.audit_log and tenant.audit_log, and the two money ledgers carry tg_append_only triggers — but audit_log immutability is enforced ONLY by revoked grants (audit_triggers=0 on the audit_log tables). So the table owner and any superuser (postgres on Supabase) can freely UPDATE/DELETE audit rows, and any future accidental re-grant defeats immutability. tg_touch_updated_at and tg_append_only are prosecdef=f (SECURITY INVOKER) — harmless today, but fragile if either is ever extended to read/write a table.
confirmed.recommendation: Attach tg_append_only (or an INSERT-only variant) to umi.audit_log and tenant.audit_log so immutability survives an accidental grant. Keep the trigger functions SECURITY INVOKER but pin their ownership to a dedicated non-superuser role in prod and keep them side-effect-free. Add a 99_verify assertion that no role holds UPDATE/DELETE on either audit_log.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: audit_triggers=0, money-ledger triggers present, both functions prosecdef=f — all reproduced. Genuine but low-severity hardening: no non-superuser role can currently mutate audit rows, so it's a robustness/defense-against-future-drift concern plus a latent SECURITY INVOKER footgun, not an exploitable path today. Low is correct.

confirmed.title: runtime schema has NO RLS while holding per-café machinery — `api` can read/write another tenant's conversations, wallet push tokens, reminders and integration cursors
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:23-29 (api full DML on all runtime, no enable-RLS loop for runtime) + 30_runtime.sql:131-175
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: 0 of 16 runtime tables have RLS (relrowsecurity=f), yet runtime holds tenant-scoped working memory keyed to café objects — runtime.conversation_state (per tenant.conversation), runtime.pass_device.push_token (per wallet pass), runtime.reminder_sent (business_id + card_id), runtime.integration_sync (per integration). api has full DML on all of them (has_table_privilege api SELECT/UPDATE on conversation_state=t; SELECT on pass_device.push_token/reminder_sent/integration_sync=t). Because runtime has zero tenant isolation, a request-path bug that passes a foreign conversation_id/pass id/business_id lets a café read or overwrite another café's live bot state, harvest another tenant's Apple/Google push tokens (to push spoofed wallet updates), read reminder history, or corrupt integration sync cursors — none of it caught by app.current_business, which no runtime policy consults.
confirmed.recommendation: Either (a) enable+force RLS on the tenant-linked runtime tables with policies that reach through to the owning business (e.g. conversation_state via tenant.conversation.business_id, pass_device via tenant.loyalty_wallet_pass→loyalty_card.business_id, reminder_sent by business_id directly, integration_sync via tenant.integration.business_id), or (b) if these are only ever touched by the BYPASSRLS worker, revoke api's DML on them entirely and route the request-path reads through business-scoped functions. Do not leave the entire runtime schema as an unscoped cross-tenant surface for the per-café role.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: This is the gap the auditor's set under-covered: findings #1/#4 address the AUTH tables in runtime, but runtime ALSO holds ordinary per-café data with no RLS and full api DML, an isolation hole of the same class as the tenant RLS work — just left entirely undone for runtime. 'Sealed' (cafés can't write) is not the same as 'tenant-isolated'; api IS a café-scoped role and here it can cross tenants inside runtime with zero DB backstop. Verified empirically. High: same cross-tenant blast radius as the tenant-RLS findings, gated only by an app bug, and push-token exfiltration enables active abuse (spoofed wallet pushes).

confirmed.title: Request-path role `api` can FORGE sessions/OTPs/reset-tokens for any user (runtime has no RLS) → passwordless account takeover of any tenant incl cross-tenant superadmin
confirmed.failure_scenario: An SQL-injection or IDOR that reaches ANY raw write on the request path lets `api` execute `INSERT INTO runtime.session(user_id, token_hash, app, expires_at) VALUES('<hola@umiconsulting.co uuid>', sha256('<attacker token>'), 'dashboard', now()+'1d')`, then present that token to authenticate as hola — who holds admin on 4 distinct tenants (verified). The same primitive forges runtime.otp (known code_hash) or runtime.password_reset_token (known token_hash), yielding full cross-tenant takeover with zero password knowledge and zero DB backstop.
confirmed.recommendation: Revoke INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on runtime.session, runtime.otp, runtime.password_reset_token, runtime.device_session, runtime.pairing from `api`; move credential-table writes to a dedicated minimally-scoped auth role via guarded SECURITY DEFINER functions. CRITICAL ADDITION: the same god-mode exists on the worker pool, which in prod connects as `postgres` (BYPASSRLS) per apps/umi-api/docs/vps-setup.md:147 — and today ALL auth writes (login credential reads, resets, session/OTP creation) run on that pool (this.pg.query). So revoking from `api` alone is necessary but INSUFFICIENT; the auth-write path must be moved off the BYPASSRLS pool onto the scoped auth role. Add a UNIQUE index on session.token_hash / otp.code_hash / password_reset_token.token_hash (verified missing: only pkey-on-id exists).
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Empirically confirmed on umi_build_v3: runtime has 16 tables with 0 RLS policies; `api` holds DELETE,INSERT,SELECT,UPDATE on all 5 auth tables and on all 16 runtime tables; session.user_id has an FK to umi.user but NO tenant/business scoping column and no CHECK; no unique index on token_hash (only session_pkey/otp_pkey/password_reset_token_pkey on id). hola@umiconsulting.co has 4 grant rows across 4 distinct tenants (verified in backfilled umi.user_role), so a single forged session = 4-tenant compromise. This is a defense-in-depth/privilege finding: weaponizing it requires a second request-path write primitive, but for a pre-cutover hardening pass over a multi-tenant DB holding money ledgers and a cross-tenant admin, the zero-backstop posture is legitimately critical. Severity retained at critical.

confirmed.title: Archival password hashes carried into umi.user are unverifiable (salt dropped) → silent lockout of all 8 password users; source also has 4 admin/staff accounts across 2 tenants sharing one hash+salt
confirmed.failure_scenario: backfill_identity carries password_hash + password_algorithm but umi.user has NO salt column (verified: 0 salt columns; source core.users.password_salt populated for all 8). password.service.verify(password, salt, hash) returns false when salt is absent and scrypt cannot be recomputed without it → if the auth module is repointed to umi.user without adding salt, all 8 hashed users are silently locked out. Separately in the SOURCE: admin@elgranribera.mx, admin@kalalacafe.mx, barista@elgranribera.mx, barista@kalalacafe.mx share a byte-identical password_hash AND password_salt (same password, spanning 2 businesses) — cracking one cracks four; 2 legacy-sha256-v1 accounts are 64-hex single-round SHA-256.
confirmed.recommendation: Do not carry the hashes: in backfill drop password_hash + password_algorithm (or NULL them), set every password user to status='invited', and force a reset/invite at cutover. This resolves BOTH the salt-loss lockout and the shared-password/legacy-hash liability at once. Do not 'fix' the lockout by re-adding a salt column and backfilling the source salt — that would re-arm the shared-password and legacy-SHA256 crackability.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Facts all verified empirically (salt populated in source for 8/8 password users; umi.user has no salt column; 4-way identical md5(password_hash) AND md5(password_salt) collision spanning El Gran Ribera + Kalala; 2 legacy 64-hex + 6 scrypt 128-hex + 1 null). Severity DOWNGRADED from critical to medium: the auditor's 'critical crackable liability' overstates build-v3 exploitability. Because the salt is dropped, the 6 scrypt hashes in umi.user are both unverifiable AND effectively un-crackable (scrypt needs the salt to brute-force). The only plausibly-crackable rows are the 2 legacy-sha256-v1 accounts, and only IF that scheme is truly unsalted — uncertain, since the source stores distinct salts for them and password.service.ts only implements scrypt (no visible legacy verifier). The 4-account shared-password is a genuine, alarming SOURCE-data hygiene fact but is inert in build-v3 (cannot authenticate, cannot crack without salt). The dominant CONFIRMED impact is availability (100% lockout of password users) plus carrying inert opaque secrets; the recommendation is correct regardless. Note: backfill comment (line 24-25) claims it drops password_algorithm but line 35 KEEPS it — harmless inconsistency.

confirmed.title: `api` and `readonly` have blanket SELECT on umi.user.password_hash and all sealed umi/runtime tables (no RLS) → any read-side leak exports every credential hash and cross-tenant data
confirmed.failure_scenario: A read-oriented IDOR/injection on the request-path `api` role, or a leak of the reporting `readonly` credential, dumps every umi.user.password_hash, every runtime session/otp/reset token_hash, and every tenant's umi rows (prospect PII, invoice, audit_log) with no row scoping — turning a minor read bug into full-platform credential + data exfiltration.
confirmed.recommendation: Replace the blanket `grant select on all tables in schema umi to api/readonly` with column-level SELECT on only the non-secret columns of umi.user (or a view), and grant table SELECT only on the catalog/entitlement objects the request path reads (channel_type, role, permission, feature, plan, plan_feature, effective_entitlement, user_role). Keep umi.audit_log, prospect, invoice, and the runtime auth-secret tables off both api and readonly.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Verified: has_column_privilege('api','umi.user','password_hash','SELECT')=true and has_column_privilege('readonly',...)=true; readonly also holds SELECT on runtime.session.token_hash and runtime.otp.code_hash; api and readonly both hold SELECT on umi.user/prospect/prospect_event/invoice/audit_log/subscription_item; umi + runtime have 0 RLS. Minor correction: umi has 16 base tables, not the 17 the auditor stated — does not change severity. High is appropriate (read-side amplification / defense-in-depth), a rung below the write-forge finding.

confirmed.title: Role/RLS-key/schema drift: the audited isolation mechanism (`SET LOCAL app.current_business` as role `api`) is not wired to the running backend — the RLS story is untested and fails closed at cutover
confirmed.failure_scenario: build-v3 policies key on current_setting('app.current_business'), but the backend (runWithTenant) sets app.tenant_id / app.user_id and connects as umi_app / (prod) postgres. Empirically, as role `api` with only app.tenant_id set (exactly what the backend sets), tenant.business returns 0 rows — RLS fails CLOSED. The backend also reads/writes build-v2 objects (tenant.login, tenant.tenant, tenant.password_reset_token, and a runtime.session with principal_type/is_active/tenant_id columns) that do not exist in build-v3's umi.user / runtime.session shape. Net effect at cutover: nothing works until roles, GUC name, and every auth/session query are reconciled — and the isolation the audit assumes has never actually been exercised.
confirmed.recommendation: Before cutover: pick ONE GUC name and grep-verify parity (change policies to app.tenant_id OR change the backend to set app.current_business); reconcile roles (either rename api/worker/readonly to umi_app/umi_worker or repoint DATABASE_URL_*), and give the request role LOGIN + a documented SCRAM password; repoint the auth module off tenant.login/tenant.password_reset_token/build-v2 runtime.session onto umi.user/runtime.*. Add 99_verify assertions that the request role is non-BYPASSRLS, that RLS returns 0 rows with no GUC, and that the policy GUC name equals the one the app sets.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Confirmed with two corrections to the auditor. (1) umi_app/umi_worker are NOT absent from build-v3 — they exist as roles, but have ZERO grants and ZERO schema USAGE on umi/tenant/runtime (verified), so the practical outcome the auditor described (nothing connects/works) is right for a different reason. (2) Fail direction: verified the request path fails CLOSED (0 rows), not open — so this is a correctness/cutover-readiness gap and 'the audited defense is unproven', NOT a data-leak vuln by itself. Severity retained at high: it is a hard cutover blocker and means none of the build-v3 RLS has ever been exercised against the real backend. Also confirmed the prod worker pool connects as `postgres` (BYPASSRLS) per vps-setup.md:147, reinforcing findings #1/#5.

confirmed.title: Password-reset single-use and expiry are app-only (no DB constraint) and run non-transactionally on the BYPASSRLS worker pool → TOCTOU token reuse
confirmed.failure_scenario: resetPassword() reads the token, checks record.usedAt/expiresAt in JS, then runs updatePassword and markResetTokenUsed as three SEPARATE statements on this.pg.query (worker pool, no BEGIN/COMMIT). Two concurrent requests replaying one still-valid token both pass the usedAt-null check before either writes used_at, so the token is honored twice. No DB backstop exists: password_reset_token has only a pkey on id (verified), no unique on token_hash, no partial-unique on used_at.
confirmed.recommendation: Make consumption atomic in one transaction: `UPDATE runtime.password_reset_token SET used_at=now() WHERE token_hash=$1 AND used_at IS NULL AND expires_at>now() RETURNING user_id` and proceed only if a row is returned. Add a UNIQUE index on token_hash. Run reset on a least-privilege auth role, not the BYPASSRLS worker pool.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Code path and non-transactional worker-pool execution confirmed in auth.service.ts:173-187 / auth.repository.ts; missing DB single-use/uniqueness confirmed empirically (only *_pkey on id). Severity medium is fair, arguably low-medium: the token is 256-bit random stored as sha256 so there is no read-leak vector, the race window is narrow (both requests must arrive before either writes used_at), and the outcome (a second reset of one's own token) is a marginal privilege gain. The real value of the finding is that it surfaces the systemic pattern — auth mutations on the BYPASSRLS pool with zero DB constraints (same root as #1). Confirmed, medium.

confirmed.title: Standing unrotated Supabase service_role/anon + Twilio secrets in git history would trump every DB control; pin TLS on the VPS→Supabase link
confirmed.location: prod-config (Supabase xbudk) + repo git history (rotation pending per project memory)
confirmed.failure_scenario: If the leaked prod Supabase service_role key is still valid, an attacker reads umi.user.password_hash and forges any runtime.session directly against the platform DB, bypassing the entire api/worker/RLS boundary this build hardens (service_role is BYPASSRLS). Separately, the VPS→Supabase connection string ('ours to configure') must pin sslmode=verify-full so it cannot fall back to cleartext/unverified TLS.
confirmed.recommendation: Before cutover, rotate the Supabase JWT secret (invalidates service_role/anon) and the Twilio auth token, and scrub/rewrite the leaked commits. Pin DATABASE_URL_* to sslmode=verify-full with the Supabase CA. Confirm umi-api never connects as service_role on any path; treat any service_role usage on the request path as a defect.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Partially verifiable from this environment. Confirmed: a role named service_role with rolbypassrls=t exists (local Supabase-emulating cluster), supporting the 'omnipotent bypass' claim in principle; password_encryption=scram-sha-256; local ssl=off. NOT verifiable from here: whether the leaked production keys are still live and whether git history still contains them — that rests entirely on project memory ('rotation pending', ROTATION PENDING note). Because the impact IF unrotated is high but the current validity is genuinely uncertain (and partly Supabase-managed, outside these SQL files), I mark this plausible rather than confirmed. Correction: the local ssl=off is a red herring for prod — Supabase enforces TLS in transit (managed); the actionable 'ours' item is pinning sslmode=verify-full on the VPS connection string, not the DB's ssl setting. Severity high, contingent on rotation not yet having happened.

confirmed.title: View tenant.conversation_analytics silently bypasses tenant RLS — any api session reads every café's conversation data
confirmed.failure_scenario: As role api with app.current_business='a13905a2-…' (El Gran Ribera): SELECT count(*) FROM tenant.conversation = 0 (RLS active) but SELECT count(*) FROM tenant.conversation_analytics = 11, ALL 11 belonging to a different business (Kalala 1860305f-…). One SELECT by any RLS-confined request returns conversation_id + business_id + outcome + turn_count + duration_seconds for every tenant, with no app-layer bug required.
confirmed.recommendation: Recreate the view WITH (security_invoker=true) so base-table RLS is evaluated against api (Supabase is PG15+); or own it by a non-BYPASSRLS role. Add a 99_verify assertion that every view in umi/tenant has security_invoker=on. Same fix applies to umi.effective_entitlement.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Metadata verified: pg_class shows view owner=juanlopez1 (superuser), reloptions=NULL (no security_invoker), and base tables conversation/message have relrowsecurity=t but relforcerowsecurity=f — so a non-materialized view resolves base scans with the superuser owner's rights and bypasses RLS. Reproduced empirically in a single transaction (set local role api + set local app.current_business): base table 0 rows, view 11 rows, all 11 for a different business. Exactly as described; high is correct (zero-effort cross-tenant read of every café's conversation metadata).

confirmed.title: runtime schema has zero RLS and full api DML; carries cross-tenant webhook metadata that api reads, and 15/16 tables cannot even be scoped (no business_id)
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/30_runtime.sql:97 (inbound_event); 90_rls.sql:26 (grant DML on all runtime to api); no RLS block for runtime
confirmed.failure_scenario: As api scoped to El Gran Ribera I read all 395 rows of runtime.inbound_event across every tenant (providers twilio + slack). Payload keys expose profile_name (WhatsApp display name), phone_hash, message_sid, slack_user_id, team_id. inbound_event/outbox_event/dead_letter carry NO business_id, so they are structurally impossible to RLS-scope; dead_letter.error is free-text and readable the same way. One IDOR/injection in the backend (which runs as api) exfiltrates every tenant's inbound event stream.
confirmed.recommendation: Do not grant api blanket DML on runtime. Give api only narrow, function-mediated (SECURITY DEFINER, scoped role) access to the specific rows a request needs; route bulk queue/webhook processing through worker only. Add business_id (or a tenant-join column) to inbound_event/outbox_event/dead_letter so RLS is even possible, then enable RLS on runtime. Store a minimal typed projection, not the raw envelope. Never surface error/last_error text to café clients.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Verified: 0/16 runtime tables have relrowsecurity; api holds SELECT+INSERT+UPDATE+DELETE on all 16 (information_schema.role_table_grants). Read all 395 inbound_event rows as scoped api. business_id column present ONLY on reminder_sent (15/16 lack it), confirming the structural point. CORRECTION to the auditor's PII claim: the twilio payload is {phone_hash, message_sid, profile_name, message_length} — the phone is HASHED and message bodies are NOT stored (only length), so the leak is cross-tenant metadata + display names + Slack workspace ids, not raw phone/message content. That narrows the PII sensitivity but not the structural defect. high stands because the schema is cross-tenant-readable with full DML and cannot be scoped without a schema change; see the separate credentials finding for the more dangerous write consequence of the same grant.

confirmed.title: api can forge auth/reset tokens for ANY user: blanket DML on runtime.session / otp / password_reset_token with no RLS
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:26 (grant insert,update,delete on all runtime to api); 30_runtime.sql:18 (session), :34 (otp), :46 (password_reset_token)
confirmed.failure_scenario: As api scoped to a café I ran: INSERT INTO runtime.session(user_id, app, token_hash, expires_at) SELECT id,'dashboard','attacker-minted', now()+interval '1 day' FROM umi.user LIMIT 1 — it SUCCEEDED, minting a session row for an arbitrary user (here the CF-dashboard/admin user 2973fcd6). The same grant allows INSERT into runtime.password_reset_token and runtime.otp for any user_id (incl Umi operators/superadmin). Any SQL-injection or IDOR in the backend (which runs as api) therefore escalates from café-scoped to full account takeover: forge a session whose token_hash = hash(a token the attacker chose), or mint a password-reset token, then authenticate as superadmin. It also allows UPDATE/DELETE of every session (mass logout / un-revoke).
confirmed.recommendation: Remove INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE (and ideally SELECT) on runtime.session, runtime.otp, runtime.password_reset_token, runtime.device_session, runtime.pairing from api. Session/token issuance must go through a narrow SECURITY DEFINER RPC owned by a dedicated auth role (or through worker), never through api's table grants. This is the same root grant as the runtime finding but a materially higher-severity consequence, so it warrants its own control.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Not in the original list — surfaced while verifying finding 2. Empirically minted a runtime.session row as scoped api and rolled back; api's INSERT privilege on otp/password_reset_token/session is confirmed by role_table_grants (all 16 runtime tables) and there is no RLS to stop it. Precondition is an app-layer bug (same as the other findings), but the blast radius is auth bypass / cross-privilege account takeover of Umi operators, so critical is warranted. This is defense-in-depth that build-v3 currently provides zero of on the auth machinery.

confirmed.title: Cross-tenant FK existence oracle and data graft: api attaches another tenant's customer to its own money-bearing card
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/20_tenant.sql:180 (loyalty_card.customer_id FK); 90_rls.sql:45-62 (business_id policy = WITH CHECK on business_id only)
confirmed.failure_scenario: As api scoped to El Gran Ribera: SELECT on the Kalala customer f036653c-… returns 0 rows (RLS), yet INSERT INTO tenant.loyalty_card(business_id=El Gran Ribera, customer_id=f036653c-…, card_number='ORACLE-TEST') SUCCEEDS — the card's WITH CHECK only tests business_id=current_business, while the customer_id FK is validated as a system operation that bypasses RLS. A random UUID instead raises foreign_key_violation naming loyalty_card_customer_id_fkey, so the same path is an existence oracle ('exists in some tenant' vs not) that also leaks the constraint name.
confirmed.recommendation: Enforce intra-tenant referential integrity: carry business_id on child tables and use composite FKs on (business_id, id) to the parent (loyalty_card → customer, ledger → card, etc.), or add BEFORE-INSERT/UPDATE triggers asserting the referenced row's business_id = current_business. The pure existence oracle is inherent to FKs, so also suppress DB error detail at the API boundary (generic 4xx, no constraint names).
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced exactly (savepoint + rollback): the graft INSERT with a literal cross-tenant UUID returned the inserted row; the random-UUID INSERT raised foreign_key_violation with the constraint name. Note the auditor's own repro subquery would have been RLS-filtered to 0 rows — the working exploit requires a LITERAL known UUID, which is precisely what the conversation_analytics view and effective_entitlement leaks hand out (v4 UUIDs are otherwise not brute-forceable). Severity medium is right: the practical graft impact is limited (money lands under the attacker's OWN business_id; the cross-tenant customer_id is a dangling reference invisible to both tenants on SELECT), so the real value is the oracle + integrity corruption, not direct fund theft.

confirmed.title: umi.effective_entitlement (and all umi tables incl user.password_hash, audit_log, prospect) exposed to any api session; view pre-bakes an RLS bypass
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/10_umi.sql:258 (view); 90_rls.sql:24 (grant select on all umi to api)
confirmed.failure_scenario: umi has zero RLS and api holds blanket SELECT, so as api scoped to El Gran Ribera I read effective_entitlement for 2 businesses / 6 rows (4 for a DIFFERENT business), leaking every café's plan/feature/limit config. The same grant lets api read all 9 umi.user rows including 8 non-null password_hash values, plus umi.audit_log (platform-privileged before/after) and umi.prospect (sales-lead PII) cross-tenant. The view is also superuser-owned without security_invoker=true, so the day umi is placed under RLS the view will silently keep returning all tenants.
confirmed.recommendation: Add security_invoker=true to effective_entitlement now so it inherits future RLS; scope entitlement reads per request (RLS on the entitlement tables keyed on app.current_business, or a business-scoped function). Remove umi.user, umi.audit_log, umi.prospect/prospect_event, umi.invoice from api's grant set entirely (worker/readonly only) — api never needs to read credential hashes or other cafés' billing/sales data.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Verified: umi has 0/16 RLS; effective_entitlement returned 6 rows across 2 distinct businesses (4 not mine) as scoped api; umi.user readable = 9 rows, 8 with non-null password_hash. View owner=juanlopez1, reloptions=NULL confirms the future-RLS-bypass point. medium is appropriate — this is cross-tenant business-confidential config + archival credential-hash exposure (offline-crackable even though the hashes are legacy scrypt/sha256 migration artifacts), not live money movement. Strengthens the KNOWN STARTING FINDING with empirical proof.

confirmed.title: Shared trigger functions set no search_path — safe today (SECURITY INVOKER) but a latent hijack if ever made SECURITY DEFINER
confirmed.failure_scenario: Both functions have prosecdef=f and proconfig=NULL. Not exploitable now (tg_touch references only now() from pg_catalog; tg_append_only calls nothing). But tg_touch_updated_at is attached by 60_triggers to every updated_at table and fires under untrusted api sessions on every UPDATE — if a future hardening pass flips it to SECURITY DEFINER, an unqualified reference resolved through a caller-controlled search_path becomes a classic definer-hijack.
confirmed.recommendation: Pin `set search_path = pg_catalog, pg_temp` (or `= ''`) on both functions now as zero-cost defense-in-depth, keep them SECURITY INVOKER unless there is a concrete reason otherwise, and fully-qualify any object references in trigger bodies.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Metadata confirms prosecdef=f and proconfig=NULL for both. Genuinely low: no current exploit path exists (invoker rights + pg_catalog-only references). It is a correct, cheap hardening recommendation, not a present vulnerability. low is the right rating.

confirmed.title: Injection surface of the build-v3 DDL/backfill is clean; residual risk is an app-layer contract on how app.current_business is set
confirmed.failure_scenario: No injectable dynamic SQL in the build: the only execute format() sites use %I with identifiers drawn from information_schema (not data); no %s, no ||-concatenated executed SQL. The backfill uses jsonb->>text (metadata->>'zettle_uuid', v->>'name', branding->>'logo_url', config->'voice'->>'tone_preset') only as VALUES flowing into data columns, never structurally; round((v->>'price')::numeric*100) is an availability risk on malformed source data, not injection. RLS scoping is a parameterizable GUC and is fail-closed. The one out-of-file expectation: the umi-api backend MUST set the business id via set_config('app.current_business',$1,true)/bound parameter, never by string-interpolating a request value into SET, or it reintroduces injection at the session-context layer.
confirmed.recommendation: Keep %I-only dynamic DDL. Document and test the backend invariant that app.current_business is set via set_config()/bound parameter with a uuid-validating middleware assertion before any query. Wrap the numeric jsonb casts in the backfill with validation/COALESCE so a bad source price fails loudly rather than aborting the cutover transaction. Separately: the coexist/backfill DB carries two LEGACY SECURITY DEFINER functions from the prod-snapshot template — confirm they are excluded from the shipped Supabase target.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Static sweep of all 8 DDL files + 8 backfill files confirms the injection characterization. GUC fail-closed verified empirically: brand-new api session with app.current_business unset → current_setting(...,true) IS NULL → tenant.customer returns 0 rows; empty string → hard 'invalid input syntax for type uuid' error. CORRECTION to the finding's 'no SECURITY DEFINER functions exist' claim: TRUE for the pristine umi_build_v3 (0 secdef funcs) and for the build-v3 DDL (only tg_touch_updated_at / tg_append_only, both invoker), but the empirical umi_backfill_v3 DOES contain two secdef functions — public.user_has_business_access[_text] (owner=juanlopez1, search_path=public, PUBLIC EXECUTE) — inherited from the umi_prod_snapshot template. They are not defined by any build-v3 file and are not carried by FDW data replay, so they won't reach the cutover target, but the orchestrator should verify that. info rating stands for the shipped build; the caveat is a housekeeping check, not a build-v3 defect.

confirmed.title: api role can dump every user's password hash + all session/OTP/reset tokens with zero DB defense-in-depth (no RLS on umi or runtime)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Confirmed on umi_backfill_v3: `SET ROLE api;` with NO app.current_business set returns 9 users and 8 password_hash values (count(password_hash)=8); runtime.session/otp/password_reset_token all read without permission error (0 rows in this fixture but access is granted). RLS coverage measured: umi 0/16, runtime 0/16, tenant 33/33. Column-grant check on umi_build_v3 shows api holds SELECT on umi.user.password_hash and password_algorithm, and DELETE/INSERT/SELECT/UPDATE on runtime.otp, runtime.session, runtime.password_reset_token. So any single request-path defect (IDOR, injection, a repo that drops the tenant predicate) running as api reads every tenant's scrypt credential archive and every live session/OTP/reset token cross-tenant, with no DB layer beneath the app to stop it.
confirmed.recommendation: Take credential/token reads off the general request path: REVOKE SELECT on umi.user.password_hash + password_algorithm and REVOKE all on runtime.otp/session/password_reset_token from api; move authentication behind a SECURITY DEFINER function (owned by a privileged role) that accepts email/user_id and returns only a verify-ok boolean or a freshly minted session, never the raw hash. Alternatively enable RLS on umi.user + runtime auth tables scoped to the authenticated principal. 99_verify.sql must assert api has NO privilege on those columns/tables.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced end-to-end on the live DBs and in the grant catalog. RLS absence on umi/runtime plus the explicit password/token grants to api are exactly as described; the blast radius (cross-tenant credential + auth-token disclosure gated only by app correctness) is real and pre-cutover. Critical is correct.

confirmed.title: Backend Postgres pool opens the VPS->Supabase link with no TLS configuration (no ssl option / no sslmode)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Both pools are `new Pool({ connectionString })` with no `ssl` object; .env.example URLs carry no `?sslmode=`; config.schema.ts only `z.string().url()`; grep of all of umi-api/src for `ssl|sslmode|rejectUnauthorized|PGSSL` returns zero matches. node-postgres does not default to TLS when sslmode is absent — it connects in cleartext, and even if SSL is negotiated it performs no certificate verification without an explicit CA/rejectUnauthorized. Because the backend self-hosts on a VPS and reaches Supabase over the public internet, an on-path attacker can capture the password hashes / session tokens exposed by finding #1 and all tenant data, or MITM by spoofing the Supabase host. This is the transport control that is entirely ours and it is unset.
confirmed.recommendation: Set `ssl: { ca: <Supabase project CA PEM>, rejectUnauthorized: true }` on both pools (or append `?sslmode=verify-full&sslrootcert=...` to each DATABASE_URL), enable Supabase 'Enforce SSL on incoming connections', and add a boot-time assertion that the negotiated connection is SSL. The gate doc must mandate verify-full and forbid require/prefer/disable.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: The code/config gap is exactly as stated and I found no compensating SSL setting anywhere in umi-api. Practical cleartext exposure is partly gated by whether Supabase 'Enforce SSL' is toggled on (managed), but the client would ship insecure-by-default and gets no cert pinning even when TLS is used, so high stands for a link carrying credentials over the internet.

confirmed.title: Nothing asserts the app pool connects as the RLS-enforced api role rather than a superuser / BYPASSRLS role
confirmed.failure_scenario: Confirmed api is rolsuper=f, rolbypassrls=f today — but the login role is whatever DATABASE_URL_APP names, and onModuleInit only runs `SELECT 1`, never checking the role's identity or attributes. 99_verify.sql asserts worker HAS bypassrls but never asserts api LACKS superuser/bypassrls. On Supabase the path-of-least-resistance string is the postgres/service_role superuser; if ops points DATABASE_URL_APP at it during the cutover scramble, all 33 tenant RLS policies are silently bypassed for every request with no error — cross-tenant reads/writes just start working.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a boot guard in PgService.onModuleInit that runs `SELECT current_user, current_setting('is_superuser'), rolbypassrls FROM pg_roles WHERE rolname=current_user` on the app pool and fails fast unless current_user='api' AND not superuser AND not bypassrls (same for readonly). Mirror the assertion in 99_verify.sql. Gate doc forbids the app/readonly pools ever using the Supabase postgres/service_role string.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Verified the current attributes are safe and that neither the boot path nor 99_verify guards against regression. It is a latent-misconfiguration guardrail rather than an active defect, but the failure mode is silent and total-RLS-bypass and the wrong-string mistake is common on Supabase, so I keep the auditor's high.

confirmed.title: Backend sets app.tenant_id / app.user_id (+ expects can_access_tenant) but build-v3 RLS reads app.current_business — setting/model drift
confirmed.failure_scenario: runWithTenant() injects set_config('app.tenant_id',...) and 'app.user_id', and comments reference an RLS `can_access_tenant` check, but every build-v3 tenant policy keys solely on current_setting('app.current_business', true) and no can_access_tenant function exists in umi_build_v3 (catalog query returned 0 rows); grep of apps/ for app.current_business returns nothing. At cutover current_setting('app.current_business', true) is NULL, `business_id = NULL::uuid` is never true, so every tenant read/write returns zero rows. Direction is fail-closed (no leak) but it is a hard cutover blocker, and the hazard is the hurried fix — loosening a policy or routing the request path through the BYPASSRLS worker (workerTx) to make rows appear, which would open cross-tenant access. The pg.service.ts header still names umi_app/umi_worker (build-v2).
confirmed.recommendation: Re-point the connection layer to the build-v3 contract before cutover: set app.current_business (matching 90_rls.sql) and connect the pools as roles api/worker (not umi_app/umi_worker). Keep set_config transaction-scoped (3rd arg true is correct). Add an integration test proving a request in tenant A's context cannot read tenant B rows through the real app pool.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Both sides verified: RLS uses only app.current_business, can_access_tenant is absent from build_v3, and the backend sets app.tenant_id/app.user_id with no app.current_business in the codebase. Fail-closed keeps it below high, but it is a genuine cutover blocker with a dangerous panic-fix path. Medium is right.

confirmed.title: Pristine 00_foundation.sql provisions only vector; the trgm search path (and other prod extensions) are absent from the from-scratch build
confirmed.failure_scenario: umi_build_v3 carries only vector (+ core plpgsql); umi_backfill_v3 carries pgcrypto, pg_trgm, unaccent, uuid-ossp, pg_stat_statements, vector — all in schema extensions. On a from-scratch Supabase build these are not auto-created. CONFIRMED real dependency: tenants.repository.ts:239 calls pg_trgm's word_similarity() and `select word_similarity(...)` errors 'function does not exist' on umi_build_v3 → tenant fuzzy-search breaks at runtime after cutover. Note a correction to the original rationale: token hashing is done in Node (node:crypto scryptSync/createHash sha256), NOT pgcrypto, so pgcrypto is not needed for auth; grep found no build-v3 use of pgcrypto, unaccent, or uuid-ossp — those appear unused. pg_stat_statements is diagnostic-only.
confirmed.recommendation: Declare in 00_foundation.sql exactly the extensions build-v3 actually uses — vector and pg_trgm (verify unaccent before relying on accent-stripping) — each `SCHEMA extensions`; drop pgcrypto/uuid-ossp (and pg_stat_statements unless deliberately kept, since SELECT on it leaks other tenants' query text). 99_verify.sql must assert the exact expected extension set and that none live in public.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Extension inventory and the word_similarity runtime break reproduced on the live DBs. The finding is real and its severity holds because a shipped feature (tenant search) breaks, but the auditor's pgcrypto/token-hashing justification is inaccurate — hashing is in Node — so the true driver is pg_trgm, and the 'drop the unused' advice is correct.

confirmed.title: No app role has USAGE on the extensions schema AND extensions is not in the roles' search_path — vector/trgm ops fail for api and worker
confirmed.location: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:12 (usage granted only on umi/tenant/runtime) + 30_runtime.sql vector columns
confirmed.failure_scenario: has_schema_privilege('api','extensions','USAGE')=f (same for worker/readonly). `SET ROLE api; SELECT '[1,2,3]'::extensions.vector` → 'permission denied for schema extensions'. Second, independent gap uncovered here: the backend references extension objects UNQUALIFIED — products.repository.ts uses `$2::vector` and the `<=>` operator, tenants.repository.ts uses bare word_similarity() — while the DDL qualifies them as extensions.*. The api/worker roles have empty rolconfig and there is no db-role search_path setting, and default search_path is "$user", public. Proven: even as superuser on umi_backfill_v3 (which HAS the extensions), unqualified `'[1,2,3]'::vector` errors 'type vector does not exist', and only resolves after `SET search_path ... , extensions`. So both embedding search and trgm search break at runtime even once USAGE is granted, unless extensions is on the search_path or the refs are schema-qualified.
confirmed.recommendation: Grant minimum privilege AND fix resolution: `GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA extensions TO api, worker` (readonly if it runs similarity), USAGE only (never CREATE); and either `ALTER ROLE api SET search_path = tenant, umi, runtime, extensions, public` (same for worker) or schema-qualify every extension reference in the backend (::extensions.vector, extensions.word_similarity). Assert both the USAGE grant and a working unqualified `::vector` cast as api in 99_verify.sql.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: USAGE denial reproduced verbatim. I also verified the auditor's fix (grant USAGE) is by itself insufficient: unqualified extension references fail on search_path resolution regardless of USAGE, so the finding is real and slightly broader than stated. Medium is appropriate — a real runtime break on two feature paths, not a security bypass.

confirmed.title: Trigger functions run with an unpinned search_path (money-ledger append-only guard included)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Both trigger functions have proconfig=NULL (no SET search_path) and are SECURITY INVOKER (prosecdef=f). App roles have no CREATE in public/umi/tenant (has_schema_privilege('api','public'|'tenant'|'umi','CREATE')=f all false), so the function-shadowing path is closed today and exploitability is low. But tg_append_only is the integrity guard on the stored-value and gift-card money ledgers; leaving its name resolution implicit is fragile — a future GRANT CREATE on public or a search_path change would silently reopen the shadowing vector.
confirmed.recommendation: Add `SET search_path = pg_catalog` (or empty search_path with fully-qualified references) to both trigger functions and to any future SECURITY DEFINER function, as standing hardening independent of current grant state.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: proconfig=NULL and SECURITY INVOKER confirmed, and the closing condition (no CREATE for app roles) confirmed, so it is correctly rated low: real defense-in-depth weakness, not currently exploitable. Worth doing because it guards the money ledgers.

confirmed.title: No prod transport/auth baseline is documented for the Supabase + self-hosted-VPS topology
confirmed.failure_scenario: grep of docs/migration/build-v3 for sslmode|pg_hba|scram|Enforce SSL|verify-full|network restriction|IP allowlist returns nothing; the artifact set is DDL + a schema verify script only, with no document stating the instance-level security contract. So the Supabase-managed vs self-managed split is undefined and managed toggles can ship at permissive defaults. Ours to set and currently unspecified: (a) client sslmode=verify-full + pinned CA (finding #2); (b) Supabase 'Enforce SSL' = ON; (c) network restriction / IP allowlist so only the VPS egress IP reaches the DB (default open); (d) app/readonly pools never use the postgres/service_role string (finding #3); (e) statement/parameter logging kept off for api so parameterized secrets are not logged.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a one-page prod transport-and-auth gate to build-v3 with pass/fail checklist items: Enforce-SSL ON; sslmode=verify-full + CA pinned; Supabase network restriction to the VPS IP; scram-sha-256 (reject md5); app pool role = api (non-super, non-bypassrls); log_statement off for api; and the exact expected extension set in schema extensions. Tie each to a 99_verify assertion where checkable in-DB.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: The absence of any transport/auth gate doc is verified by grep. It is correctly info-level: not itself a vulnerability but the missing container that leaves findings #2/#3 and the managed toggles un-owned. Endorsed as the umbrella action item.

confirmed.title: Migration-era password hashes carried: salt dropped (hashes unusable for login AND uncrackable as carried), one shared seed password across 4 admin/barista accounts on 2 tenants, 2 legacy-sha256
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified in the populated target umi_backfill_v3: 4 of 9 users (admin@elgranribera.mx, admin@kalalacafe.mx, barista@elgranribera.mx, barista@kalalacafe.mx) carry the BYTE-IDENTICAL scrypt hash 4355d447...910863 = one shared seed password reused across TWO tenants and both admin+barista; 2 more (@nectarcafe) carry legacy-sha256-v1 (64-hex bare sha256). The 32-byte per-user core.users.password_salt is NOT carried and umi.user has no salt column. has_column_privilege('api','umi.user','password_hash','SELECT') and the same for 'readonly' both return true. An api-context IDOR/SQLi or a leaked readonly reporting cred dumps all 9 hashes.
confirmed.recommendation: Set password_hash=NULL and password_algorithm=NULL for all rows and force status='invited', requiring an invite/reset for all 9 staff/operators at cutover (trivial at N=9). Separately, tell the operators to stop reusing the shared password.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Every data fact verified in the target (byte-identical shared hash, 2 legacy, salt column absent, api+readonly SELECT on password_hash). Correcting severity high->medium: the 'full offline-cracking target' claim is overstated for 6/8 hashes. Because the salt is dropped and absent from the target, the scrypt hashes are not merely unverifiable for login, they are also UNCRACKABLE as carried (you cannot compute scrypt(guess,salt) without the salt). Only the 2 legacy-sha256 hashes are plausibly fast-crackable, and only if legacy is unsalted (not determinable from data alone). What genuinely carries the severity: all 8 hashes are functionally useless post-cutover (the salt they need is gone, so nobody can even log in with them) yet remain credential-shaped liability readable by api+readonly, and the shared seed password across 4 accounts/2 tenants is a real operational-reuse problem that re-invite fixes. Recommendation is correct and cheap.

confirmed.title: Historical webhook/delivery envelopes (outbox 417 + inbound 395) re-import raw customer phone/message PII + dropped telemetry into sealed, unscoped runtime tables
