confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: runtime.outbox_event = 417 rows, 381 payloads carry a +52 recipient phone, 327 twilio.reply rows carry {to, body(full Spanish text), turn_id, trace_id} (has_to 394, has_body 394, turn_id 250, trace_id 293 — turn_id/trace_id are the comms telemetry the migration claims to DROP). runtime.inbound_event = 395 rows (all accepted), 354 carry WhatsApp profile_name. Both target tables have NO business_id and runtime has no RLS (relrowsecurity=false); readonly has SELECT, api has full DML. A leaked readonly cred or an api-context bug reads cross-tenant customer phone + message history from one unscoped table.
confirmed.recommendation: Do not migrate historical outbox_event/inbound_event at all (delivered/processed machinery; the conversation history already lives in RLS-scoped tenant.message, 1376 rows). If a redelivery-dedup window is genuinely wanted, carry only the last few days of inbound reduced to (provider, external_id, received_at) with payload=NULL; never carry outbox payloads.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: All PII/telemetry claims verified against source and confirmed loaded into the target. Correcting severity high->medium: (i) not independently exploitable — it depends on the umbrella runtime-no-RLS / api-full-DML failure; (ii) it duplicates PII that is already retained in RLS-scoped tenant.message and tenant.contact into a weaker (unscoped, sealed) location, so the delta is weaker protection rather than net-new PII; (iii) the double-send sub-claim is weak — migrated outbox rows are status sent/failed and the worker's pending path (index outbox_event_pending_idx WHERE status='pending') targets pending, so stale rows are not re-sent absent a failed-retry job (only 2 failed rows exist). Still a real data-minimization + defense-in-depth regression carrying zero forward value; recommendation sound.

confirmed.title: 435 customer dates-of-birth carried while birthday rewards are disabled on every business (data minimization)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: tenant.customer.birthday is populated for 435 of 447 customers (El Gran Ribera 352, Kalala 64, Nectar 19), while tenant.loyalty_program.birthday_reward_enabled = false for ALL 5 businesses, so the DOB corpus drives zero product function. Under an RLS bypass / IDOR that exposes tenant.customer, the leaked rows include 435 DOBs prod does not need to hold.
confirmed.recommendation: Drop birthday in the backfill (select NULL) or gate the carry on birthday_reward_enabled=true (which carries 0 rows today); re-collect only for a café that actually enables birthday rewards.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Facts verified (435/447 DOB; all 5 programs disabled; backfill carries birth_date unconditionally). Correcting severity medium->low: tenant.customer is RLS-scoped, so a café legitimately sees its OWN customers' DOBs; the incremental leak risk exists only under an RLS bypass, and DOB is moderate-sensitivity PII. It is a genuine data-minimization/compliance improvement (holding sensitive PII with zero product use) but low exploit impact.

confirmed.title: Money ledger external_ref = 348 dangling umi-cash cuids (mislabeled 'Zettle uuid'), redundant with idempotency_key
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: all 348 non-null external_ref values sit on reason='migration_initial_balance' rows and are umi-cash Prisma cuids (source_type='cash_loyalty_card', e.g. cmn9hvbyi000x5birme00df37), NOT Zettle payment uuids as the 20_tenant.sql:200 comment asserts. The same rows already carry idempotency_key='migrate_cash_card_*', and the 2 real purchase + 2 real topup rows carry NO external_ref — so external_ref currently holds ONLY mislabeled dead pointers into the decommissioned umi-cash DB.
confirmed.recommendation: Set external_ref=NULL for reason='migration_initial_balance' rows; reserve external_ref for genuine Zettle refs on real purchase/topup/refund rows. idempotency_key already guarantees the initial balance is applied once.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Verified in the target: 348/348 migration rows carry both external_ref (cuid) and idempotency_key; real purchase/topup rows carry neither. Data-hygiene/provenance nit plus a mislabeled-column comment that will mislead future reconciliation code; not a leak. Low is correct.

confirmed.title: Synthetic ghost account (@umi.invalid, null password) carried as status='active' holding an admin grant on Kalala
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: the CF-dashboard placeholder user is carried with email 'migrated+2973fcd6...@umi.invalid', password_hash NULL, status='active', and holds an admin grant on Kalala Café — it is Kalala's 3rd admin alongside admin@kalalacafe.mx and superadmin hola@umiconsulting.co. If any auth path treats a null-password/invalid-email account as anything other than 'cannot log in' (magic-link, SSO auto-provision, reset-to-set-initial), it hands out admin, and it silently inflates the admin roster.
confirmed.recommendation: Carry the row to keep the user_role FK intact, but insert it with status='suspended' (or 'invited'), not 'active'. Confirm no cutover auth flow can activate a null-password account, and review whether the grant is still wanted.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: The data facts are certain (status='active', null hash, admin on Kalala, roster now 3). Marked plausible and severity medium->low because exploitability is contingent on auth-flow behavior not present in these files: the email is a non-routable @umi.invalid so password-reset/magic-link cannot deliver, and post-cutover email+hash login fails on a null hash — so 'hands out admin' requires a future SSO/auto-provision path. The fix (carry as suspended) is cheap, sound defense-in-depth.

confirmed.title: 398 live wallet push tokens carried into runtime.pass_device while the sibling Apple auth_token was dropped as 'regenerated'
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: all 398 pass_device rows carry a non-empty push_token, while backfill_loyalty.sql:155 deliberately DROPS passes.auth_token (350 non-empty) as 'regenerated' post-cutover. Both are pass-service artifacts that re-register on the first device callback after cutover, so the carry is inconsistent; push_token sits in unscoped runtime readable by api/worker/readonly.
confirmed.recommendation: Drop push_token on carry (it re-registers on first pass web-service callback, same rationale used for auth_token) or, if push continuity matters, treat it as a secret readable only by the wallet worker.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Facts verified (398/398 non-empty push_token carried; auth_token dropped). Keep low but the stated impact is overstated: a push_token alone does NOT let an arbitrary attacker 'spam 398 phones' — sending a wallet push also requires Umi's APNs auth key / FCM server key (held server-side), so a token leak is not directly abusable. The valid, load-bearing point is minimization/consistency (carry both or neither).

confirmed.title: tenant.audit_log before/after re-imports config fields the clean model dropped, and leaks a Umi-internal Slack channel id into a café-readable log
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: all 20 migrated audit rows carry full before/after config snapshots including payment_methods (20), whatsapp (20) and special_notice (20) — fields the commerce backfill deliberately did NOT model on tenant.business — as opaque jsonb in an RLS-scoped, café-readable audit_log; 1 row embeds Umi-internal slack_channel_id='C0AJJEC73K7' / slack_channel_name='kalala-café-chapule' inside new_config. A café owner reading their own audit trail sees Umi-internal ops identifiers and a year-old snapshot of dropped config.
confirmed.recommendation: Scrub Umi-internal keys (slack_channel_id/name) from before/after before insert; note these blobs are the de-facto retention home for config you chose not to model; redact payment_methods values if minimization matters.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Verified. Note the actor_slack_id COLUMN is correctly dropped (backfill sets actor_user_id NULL and does not carry actor_slack_id) — the leak is specifically the slack_channel_id/name embedded in the new_config jsonb that rides into before/after. A Slack channel id is an internal identifier, not a usable credential, so impact is low; low is correct.

confirmed.title: Contact phone PII duplicated across raw_phone_number and normalized_value in 432/458 rows
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: of 458 contacts, 432 store the identical +52 phone in both raw_phone_number and normalized_value (26 differ by formatting; 0 raw null; raw_value used = 0). Not a cross-tenant leak, but any future redaction/tokenization/erasure of phone PII must cover BOTH columns (and contact_lookup_idx on (business_id, normalized_value)) or the redundant copy silently defeats it.
confirmed.recommendation: Keep the phone (product-needed loyalty soft-key) but document that raw_phone_number and normalized_value are one PII field stored twice, and ensure any masking/erasure routine targets both columns and the lookup index.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Verified in the target (458 total, 432 equal, raw_value unused). Info-level data-modeling/hygiene note; correctly rated.

confirmed.title: Customer message-content embeddings (1342) carried into unscoped, sealed runtime.message_embedding — invertible PII at rest with no tenant isolation (NEW, beyond the supplied set)
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified: 1342 vectors derived from customer WhatsApp message bodies land in runtime.message_embedding, which has no business_id, no RLS (relrowsecurity=false), and readonly SELECT. Embedding-inversion techniques can partially reconstruct source text, so the same cross-tenant, unscoped exposure path as the outbox/inbound finding applies to the vectors: a leaked readonly cred or an api bug reads all tenants' message embeddings from one table.
confirmed.recommendation: Treat this only under the sealed-service trust boundary; at minimum revoke readonly SELECT on runtime.*_embedding, and if runtime ever gains tenant scoping include message_embedding. It cannot simply be dropped like the outbox because it has legitimate RAG read-back utility.
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Newly identified in this review. Overlaps the umbrella runtime-no-RLS finding rather than being independent, and impact is lower than the outbox finding because embeddings are only partially invertible and the table has genuine forward utility (so it cannot be dropped). Included because it extends the customer-PII-at-rest surface the original set missed.

fixes.summary: The build-v3 RLS model has a correct spine (33/33 tenant tables enable RLS with a business-scoped policy, verified to block cross-tenant child writes) but four structural gaps make it bypassable with zero or one app-layer bug: (1) both views (tenant.conversation_analytics, umi.effective_entitlement) are non-security_invoker and owner-resolved, so they run with the superuser owner's rights and leak every café's rows — reproduced live (base table 0 rows vs view 11 cross-tenant rows); (2) no tenant table has FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, so the owner (and any request that lands on the owner/BYPASSRLS role) bypasses all isolation — this is the root enabler of the view leak; (3) the umi schema has no RLS and api holds blanket SELECT including umi.user.password_hash, umi.audit_log and umi.prospect; (4) the runtime schema has no RLS and api holds full DML on all 16 tables including otp / password_reset_token / session / conversation_state / pass_device.push_token — a cross-tenant + credential surface gated only by app correctness. Separately, RBAC (user_role / role_permission / permission) is stored but never consulted by any policy and role_permission has 0 rows, so all authorization rests on one app-set GUC (app.current_business) with no DB backstop. The fixes below are ordered by leverage: security_invoker on the two views + FORCE on tenant close the confirmed leak; a least-privilege grant rewrite + targeted umi/runtime RLS removes the credential/cross-tenant surface; a SECURITY DEFINER business-context gate binds the session to an entitled principal; a BEFORE trigger closes the cross-tenant FK graft/oracle. Every fix goes into 90_rls.sql / 60_triggers.sql (which run AFTER the superuser backfill) or the two view definitions, so none can break the backfill; each ships with a 99_verify gate assertion.

changes.what: Recreate both cross-tenant-leaking views WITH (security_invoker = true) so underlying-table RLS is evaluated as the calling api role, not the (superuser/postgres) view owner. Closes the directly-exploitable confirmed leak in tenant.conversation_analytics and pre-empts the identical latent leak in umi.effective_entitlement once umi gains RLS.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/20_tenant.sql:502 (create view tenant.conversation_analytics) and docs/migration/build-v3/10_umi.sql:258 (create view umi.effective_entitlement)
changes.sql_or_config:
-- 20_tenant.sql (replace the CREATE VIEW header only; body unchanged)
create view tenant.conversation_analytics
  with (security_invoker = true) as
  select c.id as conversation_id, c.business_id, c.outcome,
         count(m.id) as turn_count,
         extract(epoch from (max(m.occurred_at) - c.started_at))::int as duration_seconds
    from tenant.conversation c
    left join tenant.message m on m.conversation_id = c.id
   group by c.id, c.business_id, c.outcome, c.started_at;

-- 10_umi.sql (replace the CREATE VIEW header only; body unchanged)
create view umi.effective_entitlement
  with (security_invoker = true) as
  select ... (existing 10_umi.sql:258-286 body verbatim) ...;
changes.rationale: A normal (non-materialized) view runs SELECTs against its base tables using the privileges and RLS context of the view's OWNER unless security_invoker is set; because both views are owned by a superuser locally (postgres/supabase_admin in prod) that owner is exempt from the base tables' non-forced RLS, so isolation is skipped inside the view. PostgreSQL 15 added the security_invoker view option precisely for this: with it true, permission checks AND row-security policies on referenced objects use the invoking user (api) — the RLS the base tables already carry then applies. api already holds SELECT on the referenced base tables (tenant.conversation/message; umi.subscription/plan_feature/feature/entitlement_override kept below), so no additional grants are needed. Supabase is PG15+ and local is PG18, so the option is available in both. Ref: PostgreSQL docs, CREATE VIEW 'security_invoker'; §5.9 Row Security Policies (views).
changes.verification: Gate 1 (metadata, add to 99_verify.sql): SELECT count(*) FROM pg_class c JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid=c.relnamespace WHERE c.relkind='v' AND n.nspname IN ('umi','tenant') AND coalesce(array_to_string(c.reloptions,','),'') NOT LIKE '%security_invoker=true%'  ==> must be 0 (else RAISE). Gate 2 (behavioral, reproduces the confirmed leak): in one txn: SET LOCAL ROLE api; SET LOCAL app.current_business='a13905a2-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'; SELECT count(*) FROM tenant.conversation_analytics ==> must equal SELECT count(*) FROM tenant.conversation (both 0), NOT 11.

changes.what: Add FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY to every RLS-enabled tenant base table. Removes the owner-bypass path that is the root cause of the view leak and of silent isolation loss if any request-path connection ever runs as the table owner.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql — append after the existing enable/policy blocks (after line 129). Runs after the backfill, so it cannot affect data load.
changes.rationale: ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY leaves the table OWNER exempt from policies; FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY makes policies apply to the owner too. Superusers and BYPASSRLS roles (worker) still bypass, so this is safe for the worker and for the superuser backfill, but it eliminates the entire class of 'a query ran as the owner and saw everything' bugs — including the view leak (with security_invoker the scan runs as api; with FORCE even an owner-context scan is contained) and future misconfig where api is made the owner or a pooled DATABASE_URL points the request path at the owner. It is a one-line-per-table, defense-in-depth control with no behavior change for the intended non-owner api path (already contained) and non-owner readonly path. Ref: PostgreSQL docs, ALTER TABLE 'FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY'; §5.9 (table owners bypass unless FORCE; superuser/BYPASSRLS always bypass). CIS PostgreSQL Benchmark §6 (row security) — enforce RLS for all roles including owner.
changes.verification: Add to 99_verify.sql: SELECT count(*) FROM pg_class c JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid=c.relnamespace WHERE n.nspname='tenant' AND c.relkind='r' AND c.relrowsecurity AND NOT c.relforcerowsecurity  ==> must be 0 (currently 33). Behavioral: as the table owner (non-superuser test role) SET app.current_business to business A then SELECT from tenant.conversation returns only A's rows, not all.

changes.what: Narrow the blanket table grants so api gets SELECT-only on views (it currently also holds INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on tenant.conversation_analytics via the `on all tables` grant). Views should never be writable by the tenant role.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:25 (grant select,insert,update,delete on all tables in schema tenant to api) — add a corrective revoke immediately after the tenant grant block.
changes.sql_or_config:
-- Views are read surfaces; strip the DML the blanket grant handed api.
revoke insert, update, delete on tenant.conversation_analytics from api;
-- (umi.effective_entitlement already only received SELECT because api's umi grant is SELECT-only; the umi rewrite below keeps it SELECT-only.)
-- Preferred: grant per object-type instead of `on all tables` so future views never inherit DML:
--   grant select on all tables in schema tenant to api;  (then re-grant DML on BASE TABLES only, or revoke on views explicitly)
changes.rationale: GRANT ... ON ALL TABLES includes views; api thus received INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on conversation_analytics. Even though the view is not auto-updatable today (writes would error), granting write on a reporting view violates least privilege and would silently become live if the view were ever made updatable or backed by INSTEAD OF triggers. Least-privilege grants are CIS PostgreSQL Benchmark §4 (revoke excessive/default privileges) and OWASP A01 (Broken Access Control). Ref: PostgreSQL docs, GRANT (privileges apply to views); CREATE VIEW (updatable views).

changes.what: Rewrite api's umi grants to least privilege: remove SELECT on credentials/audit/sales entirely (umi.user, umi.audit_log, umi.prospect, umi.prospect_event), keep the café-shared global catalogs readable, and RLS-scope the per-café entitlement/billing tables so api reads only its current business. Also stop future umi tables from auto-exposing to api. Includes the explicit archival-password-hash decision.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:24 (grant select on all tables in schema umi to api) and :27 (alter default privileges ... grant select ... to api) — replace both.
changes.rationale: api is a per-café request role; it never needs another admin's password material, Umi's platform audit trail, or unrelated sales leads. Removing those grants is CIS PostgreSQL Benchmark §4.7-4.8 (revoke excessive privileges / restrict SELECT on sensitive columns) and OWASP A01/A02. Scoping the per-café entitlement/billing tables with RLS on app.current_business keeps the security_invoker effective_entitlement view working (api scans subscription/entitlement_override under RLS -> only its own rows) while blocking the confirmed cross-business read (6 rows / 2 businesses -> 1 business). ARCHIVAL PASSWORD-HASH DECISION (explicit): umi.user holds 9 staff/operator rows carried from the umi-cash->platform migration — 6 bare-hex scrypt-sha256-v1, 2 legacy-sha256-v1, 1 null (invited). (a) The request-path role must have ZERO read on password_hash/password_algorithm: login verification must run through a SECURITY DEFINER function (or a dedicated unauthenticated `auth` role) that compares inside the DB and returns a session, never the hash — so revoking api SELECT on umi.user does not break login, it relocates it. (b) The 2 legacy-sha256-v1 hashes are fast, effectively unsalted, and offline-crackable; they should be invalidated at cutover (blank the hash, force password-reset) rather than left at rest, and the scrypt rows upgraded-on-next-login to the current scheme. That rotation is a data-migration follow-up (not applied here), but the grant-removal is the load-bearing containment: even an IDOR/injection in api can no longer exfiltrate any hash. Ref: OWASP ASVS V2/V6 (credential storage/least exposure); PostgreSQL GRANT column privileges.
changes.verification: Add to 99_verify.sql: assert has_table_privilege('api','umi.user','SELECT') = false AND has_table_privilege('api','umi.audit_log','SELECT') = false AND has_table_privilege('api','umi.prospect','SELECT') = false AND has_table_privilege('api','umi.plan','SELECT') = true (catalog still readable). Behavioral: SET LOCAL ROLE api; SET LOCAL app.current_business='<business A>'; SELECT count(DISTINCT business_id) FROM umi.effective_entitlement  ==> 1 (only A), and SELECT count(*) FROM umi.user raises 'permission denied'.

changes.what: Give the runtime schema tenant isolation + least privilege: default-deny api DML on runtime, enable+FORCE reach-through RLS on the 9 tenant-linked runtime tables, re-grant api only the reach-through tables it serves, route auth/queue machinery through worker/SECURITY DEFINER, and revoke readonly's blanket read of the invertible message/product/knowledge embeddings.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:26 and :29 (api full DML on all runtime + default privileges) — replace; add a new runtime RLS section after the tenant blocks.
changes.rationale: 'Sealed' (cafés cannot write business facts here) is not 'tenant-isolated': api is a café-scoped role and today it can read/overwrite any tenant's live bot state, harvest any tenant's Apple/Google push_token (enabling spoofed wallet pushes), read reminder history and corrupt integration cursors, none of it consulted by app.current_business. Default-deny then reach-through RLS applies the same isolation model the tenant schema already uses, keyed on the same GUC, so a request-path bug that passes a foreign conversation_id/pass id/business_id is contained by the DB. Auth tables (session/otp/password_reset_token) are user-scoped credential material with no business dimension; the correct control is to keep them off the tenant role and expose only verb-shaped SECURITY DEFINER functions (issue/verify/consume) — OWASP A01 (function-level authorization) + least privilege. Queue tables are worker machinery. The 1342 message embeddings are partially invertible (embedding-inversion research) customer WhatsApp content, so readonly's blanket SELECT is dropped while the tables keep their RAG utility. Ref: PostgreSQL §5.9 RLS; CIS PostgreSQL §4/§6; OWASP A01/A04.
changes.verification: Add to 99_verify.sql: SELECT count(*) FROM pg_class c JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid=c.relnamespace WHERE n.nspname='runtime' AND c.relkind='r' AND c.relname IN ('conversation_state','pass_device','reminder_sent','integration_sync','device_session','pairing','product_embedding','message_embedding','knowledge_embedding') AND NOT (c.relrowsecurity AND c.relforcerowsecurity)  ==> must be 0. assert has_table_privilege('readonly','runtime.message_embedding','SELECT') = false. Behavioral: SET LOCAL ROLE api; SET LOCAL app.current_business='a13905a2-...(business with 0 conversations)'; SELECT count(*) FROM runtime.conversation_state ==> 0 (not the Kalala rows).

changes.what: Bind the RLS session to an entitled principal: add an app.current_user GUC and a SECURITY DEFINER choke-point that verifies the user holds a user_role grant for the business BEFORE the app.current_business GUC is set, plus a permission-check helper for privileged writes, and seed the currently-empty role_permission map. Gives the DB a real authorization backstop instead of trusting a single app-set GUC.
changes.where: New file docs/migration/build-v3/40_authz.sql (run after 10_umi, before 90_rls) or appended to 90_rls.sql. Wiring note for the umi-api backend request path.
changes.rationale: Every tenant/runtime policy trusts app.current_business alone; the DB verifies only that SOME business id was set, never that the authenticated principal holds a grant for it, and never checks any permission key — and role_permission has 0 rows, so the whole permission model is inert. A tenant-id-from-request bug or a stale GUC on a pooled connection therefore serves/mutates a tenant the user is not entitled to. Centralizing entry through a SECURITY DEFINER gate (owned by a role that can read user_role) gives one enforced choke-point with a DB backstop, far cheaper than adding an EXISTS subquery to 40+ policies, and follows the established Supabase/Postgres pattern of SECURITY DEFINER authorization helpers. app.current_user must be derived from the verified session server-side, never from client input (OWASP A01/A08). search_path is pinned and PUBLIC execute revoked to avoid the CVE-2018-1058 / function-hijack class. STABLE + an index on user_role(user_id, business_id) keeps it cheap. Ref: PostgreSQL CREATE FUNCTION (SECURITY DEFINER, search_path); §5.9; OWASP A01 Broken Access Control / A04 Insecure Design.
changes.verification: Add to 99_verify.sql: assert (SELECT count(*) FROM umi.role_permission) > 0 (RBAC wired); assert to_regprocedure('umi.set_business_context(uuid)') IS NOT NULL. Behavioral gate: SET LOCAL ROLE api; SET LOCAL app.current_user='<user granted only business A>'; SELECT umi.set_business_context('<business B>')  ==> must RAISE errcode 42501; SELECT umi.set_business_context('<business A>') ==> succeeds and current_setting('app.current_business') = A.

changes.what: Close the cross-tenant FK graft/oracle: block attaching another tenant's customer to a local money-bearing loyalty_card via a BEFORE trigger that asserts the referenced row belongs to the current business, and suppress DB constraint detail at the API boundary so the FK existence oracle stops leaking. Apply the same pattern to any other child->cross-tenant-parent reference.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/60_triggers.sql (new trigger) covering the FK at docs/migration/build-v3/20_tenant.sql:180 (tenant.loyalty_card.customer_id); repeat for analogous parent references. Plus an API-boundary error-handling note.
changes.rationale: Confirmed: as api scoped to café A, an INSERT into loyalty_card with a literal café-B customer_id SUCCEEDS (the child WITH CHECK tests only business_id; the FK target is validated as a system operation that bypasses RLS), while a random UUID raises foreign_key_violation naming the constraint — an existence oracle that turns any leaked UUID (e.g. from the view leaks above) into confirmed cross-tenant knowledge and grafts a dangling cross-tenant reference onto a money-bearing row. Running the check as a SECURITY INVOKER trigger means the referenced customer is resolved under the caller's RLS, so a cross-tenant row is simply not visible and the insert is refused — no separate business_id comparison needed. Suppressing constraint/SQLSTATE detail at the API boundary is OWASP A01/A05 (improper error handling / IDOR) and removes the inherent FK oracle that a trigger alone cannot (the timing/behavior difference is intrinsic to FKs). Ref: PostgreSQL §5.9 (FK checks run as a system operation, not under RLS); OWASP A01 Broken Access Control.
changes.verification: Behavioral gate (savepoint + rollback): SET LOCAL ROLE api; SET LOCAL app.current_business='<café A>'; INSERT INTO tenant.loyalty_card(business_id, customer_id, card_number) VALUES ('<A>', '<a café-B customer uuid>', 'ORACLE-TEST')  ==> must RAISE (currently returns the inserted row). Control: the same INSERT with an A-owned customer_id succeeds. API-layer test: a request that triggers a FK/RLS error returns a generic 4xx with no constraint name / SQLSTATE in the body.

fixes.research_basis: PostgreSQL 16/18 documentation: §5.9 Row Security Policies (ENABLE vs FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; table owners bypass unless FORCE; superusers and BYPASSRLS roles always bypass; FK/uniqueness checks are system operations not subject to RLS; views and RLS). CREATE VIEW — the `security_invoker` storage option (added in PostgreSQL 15; makes base-relation permission checks AND row-security policies use the invoking user rather than the view owner). CREATE FUNCTION — SECURITY DEFINER semantics and the mandatory `SET search_path` hardening (function-hijack / CVE-2018-1058 pattern), REVOKE ... FROM PUBLIC on definer functions. GRANT/REVOKE — privileges apply to views the same as tables; ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES governs future objects; column-level privileges for sensitive columns. CIS PostgreSQL Benchmark: §4 (revoke excessive/default and PUBLIC privileges; restrict access to sensitive data), §6 (Row-Level Security enforcement for all roles including the owner), §7 (role attributes / least privilege). OWASP Top 10 2021: A01 Broken Access Control (IDOR, missing function/object-level authorization, principal-to-tenant binding from trusted session not request input), A04 Insecure Design (defense-in-depth, fail-closed context entry), A05 Security Misconfiguration (leaking DB error/constraint detail to clients). OWASP ASVS V1/V2/V4/V6 (access-control architecture, credential storage and least exposure). Embedding-inversion literature (text partially reconstructable from sentence embeddings) motivates treating runtime.*_embedding as customer PII at rest. Empirical grounding: reproduced against live umi_backfill_v3 (PG18) — conversation_analytics view returns 11 cross-tenant rows while the RLS'd base table returns 0; 33/33 tenant tables relrowsecurity=t but relforcerowsecurity=f; umi 0/16 and runtime 0/16 RLS; api holds SELECT on umi.user (incl. 8 non-null legacy password_hash) + full DML on all 16 runtime tables + INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on the analytics view; role_permission=0 rows; backfill runner (00_run_backfill.sh) applies 90_rls.sql AFTER data load and connects as superuser, so FORCE/security_invoker cannot affect the backfill; Supabase prod is PG15+, so security_invoker is available in both environments.
fixes.risks: App-integration dependency (gate before cutover, not applied here): revoking api's read on umi.user and its DML on runtime auth tables (session/otp/password_reset_token) WILL break login/session flows IF the umi-api request path currently connects as `api` and reads those rows directly. The fix presumes login is relocated to SECURITY DEFINER functions or a dedicated unauthenticated `auth` role; this must be confirmed against the self-hosted umi-api before applying, or login fails closed. Similarly, the new SECURITY DEFINER context gate requires the backend to SET app.current_user (from the verified session, never request input) and call umi.set_business_context() instead of a bare SET — a coordinated backend change; until then, ship the functions but keep the app path working. Readonly reporting: enabling RLS on umi per-café tables (and the existing tenant/runtime RLS) filters the non-BYPASSRLS `readonly` role to whatever app.current_business is set — cross-tenant reporting already requires per-business context or a BYPASSRLS reporting role under this model; do not grant readonly BYPASSRLS as a shortcut (it would re-open cross-tenant read). FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is safe for the superuser backfill and BYPASSRLS worker, but if the PROD object owner on Supabase is a plain (non-BYPASSRLS, non-superuser) role, forced RLS will also filter owner-run maintenance/migration queries — verify the prod owner's rolbypassrls/rolsuper before relying on owner access, and run DDL/maintenance as a bypass role. Performance: the reach-through EXISTS policies and the per-request authz function add lookups; add supporting indexes (user_role(user_id, business_id); the reach-through FKs are already indexed as PKs/FKs) and keep the helpers STABLE so they are evaluated once per statement. security_invoker views require the invoker to hold SELECT on every referenced base table — verified true for api on the tenant view and preserved for the umi view by the entitlement grants above; re-check if either view body is later extended to a table api lacks. The FK existence oracle is intrinsic to foreign keys — the trigger blocks the graft and the same-tenant timing case, but only API-boundary generic error handling removes the residual timing/behavior oracle. Scope note: TLS/SCRAM/pg_hba are partly Supabase-managed and out of this RLS area; the grant/RLS/trigger changes here are entirely 'ours to configure' in 90_rls.sql / 60_triggers.sql / the two view definitions / a new 40_authz.sql, all of which run after the backfill so none can corrupt the data load.

fixes.summary: The build-v3 grant model in 90_rls.sql hands the RLS-enforced request-path role `api` and the reporting role `readonly` far more than they need: `api` holds full INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on every runtime auth table (session/otp/password_reset_token/device_session/pairing) plus DML on views, `api`+`readonly` can SELECT umi.user.password_hash and every runtime token/code hash, `worker` (BYPASSRLS) can mint platform-wide grants via umi.user_role, and ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES auto-arms api/worker on every future table. All ten findings reproduced on umi_build_v3 and umi_backfill_v3. The fix is a defense-in-depth rewrite of the grant block: replace schema-wide sweeps with per-table + column-level grants that exclude the secret surface; move the credential/session lifecycle onto a dedicated `authsvc` principal reached only through SECURITY DEFINER functions (so a request-path SQLi/IDOR can no longer forge a session for the cross-tenant superadmin hola@umiconsulting.co, who holds admin on 4 businesses); scope defaults so least-privilege can't silently regress; add a boot guard + verify assertion that the app pool is never a superuser/BYPASSRLS role; grant extensions USAGE and pin search_path so vector/trgm search works; add unique indexes on the high-entropy token hashes; and carry the null-password ghost account as suspended. None of this touches the tenant RLS policies or the superuser-run backfill. Everything is proposal-only; nothing was applied.

changes.what: Kill the request-path forge primitive: strip ALL DML on the runtime auth tables from `api` and move the credential/session lifecycle onto a dedicated `authsvc` principal exposed only via SECURITY DEFINER functions. (Merges the two critical findings F2/F6 and the high F3 — one root over-grant on 90_rls.sql:26.)
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/00_foundation.sql (add `authsvc` role beside api/worker/readonly); docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql (replace the blanket `grant select,insert,update,delete on all tables in schema runtime to api`, line 26); new auth surface appended to docs/migration/build-v3/30_runtime.sql; backend: apps/umi-api auth module must call login/reset/session-mint on a SEPARATE pool connecting as authsvc, never on the api pool.
changes.rationale: Verified on both DBs: has_table_privilege('api','runtime.session','INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE')=t, same for otp/password_reset_token, and has_column_privilege('api','runtime.session','token_hash','SELECT')=t. `api` runs EVERY café request, so one SQLi/session-minting bug lets it `INSERT INTO runtime.session(user_id, token_hash, app, expires_at) VALUES('<hola uuid>', <known hash>, 'dashboard', now()+'1y')` and authenticate as a 4-tenant superadmin. A request-path role must not own the auth-material lifecycle (PostgreSQL least-privilege; CIS PostgreSQL Benchmark 'assign least privilege to application roles'). SECURITY DEFINER with a pinned `search_path` (PostgreSQL 'Writing SECURITY DEFINER Functions Safely' — guards the trojan search-path attack) lets api validate a session without SELECT on token_hash, and role separation shrinks the forge blast radius from 'any request-path bug' to 'the small, heavily-reviewed auth module only'. RISK: larger backend change — the auth module must use a distinct pool as `authsvc` (a real LOGIN role with its own rotated secret on Supabase) and session validation becomes an RPC; scrypt verification stays in Node, so authsvc still exposes password_hash TO THE AUTH MODULE (the minimized, intended surface). Revoke-only without moving the write path off the current `postgres`/BYPASSRLS worker pool is necessary but insufficient.
changes.verification: SET-based gate (expect t): select not (has_table_privilege('api','runtime.session','INSERT') or has_table_privilege('api','runtime.session','UPDATE') or has_table_privilege('api','runtime.session','DELETE') or has_table_privilege('api','runtime.otp','INSERT') or has_table_privilege('api','runtime.password_reset_token','UPDATE') or has_column_privilege('api','runtime.session','token_hash','SELECT')) and has_function_privilege('api','runtime.session_principal(text)','EXECUTE') as pass;

changes.what: Remove SELECT on umi.user credential columns from `api` and `readonly`; replace the blanket umi SELECT to api with per-table grants on the global catalogs + grant-graph + the resolved-entitlement view, locking per-café billing/PII/audit tables away from the request path.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql: replace `grant select on all tables in schema umi to api` (line 24); add a readonly carve-out after line 15.
changes.sql_or_config:
-- 90_rls.sql: replace line 24.
-- Global catalogs + grant graph + resolved entitlement — full SELECT is safe:
grant select on
  umi.channel_type, umi.role, umi.role_permission, umi.permission,
  umi.feature, umi.plan, umi.plan_feature, umi.user_role,
  umi.effective_entitlement                 -- view runs as owner (security_invoker off, verified) → resolves subscription/override with NO base access
  to api;
-- Identity WITHOUT the credential columns:
grant select (id, email, full_name, status, last_login_at, created_at, updated_at)
  on umi.user to api;
-- Intentionally NOT granted to api: umi.subscription, subscription_item, invoice,
--   entitlement_override, prospect, prospect_event, audit_log.

-- readonly: keep its broad reporting SELECT but carve out the credential columns.
revoke select on umi.user from readonly;
grant select (id, email, full_name, status, last_login_at, created_at, updated_at)
  on umi.user to readonly;
changes.rationale: Verified: has_column_privilege('api'/'readonly','umi.user','password_hash','SELECT')=true. Column-level SELECT is the native least-privilege mechanism (PostgreSQL 'Privileges'/GRANT column list); credential hashes must never sit in a request-path or reporting read surface (OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet). The effective_entitlement view has empty reloptions → security_invoker=false → it executes with the owner's rights, so granting api SELECT on the VIEW resolves each café's entitlement without any base grant on umi.subscription/entitlement_override/plan_feature, keeping billing tables locked (the view comment already says 'query this, never plan_feature directly'). RISK: any code doing `select *` from umi.user breaks (must enumerate columns; count(*) still works). The password-verify path must read the hash via authsvc (change 1). api loses base SELECT on umi.subscription/invoice — a dashboard billing screen must route through worker/authsvc or a dedicated hash-free view; readonly keeps subscription/invoice/plan for revenue reporting (only umi.user is carved).

changes.what: Add UNIQUE indexes on the high-entropy token hashes (session, password_reset_token, device_session); deliberately do NOT unique-index the low-entropy otp.code_hash / pairing.code — index them by user/device scope instead.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/30_runtime.sql (with each table's index definitions).
changes.rationale: Verified only pkey-on-id exists on these tables (session_pkey/otp_pkey/password_reset_token_pkey on id). A presented bearer/reset token must map to at most ONE row; a unique index turns token-hash collision/forgery into a constraint violation and makes the lookup an index scan. Correctness caveat (refines the raw finding, which suggested a unique index on otp.code_hash): uniqueness is correct ONLY for high-entropy random tokens; forcing it on low-entropy OTP/pairing codes would break legitimate duplicate codes across users — hence the split. Defense-in-depth around the auth substrate (OWASP session-management: unpredictable, single-mapping session identifiers). RISK: if any backfilled/existing row shares a token_hash the CREATE UNIQUE fails — none expected (these runtime tables are empty at cutover; backfill does not populate them); negligible write overhead.

-- backfill_identity.sql: replace the status CASE (lines 37-39) so a null hash can never be 'active'.

         when u.password_hash is null then 'suspended'   -- cannot password-login; not a live account
         when u.status = 'active'      then 'active'
         when u.status = 'invited'     then 'invited'
         else 'suspended'
       end,
-- The @umi.invalid ghost (umi.user 2973fcd6-2929-6d9d-fc10-940ff2708145) is still carried so its
-- umi.user_role FK to Kalala holds, but as 'suspended': it stops being Kalala's 3rd 'admin' and can
-- never be activated by a future SSO/auto-provision path. Separately review whether its admin-on-Kalala

ARCHIVAL-HASH DECISION (explicit): the 8 non-null hashes are bare-hex from the umi-cash migration (scrypt-sha256-v1 x6, legacy-sha256-v1 x2) — KEEP them plus the opaque password_algorithm so post-cutover email+password login is seamless (dropping them would force 8 resets, and the reset path must be hardened first). Treat legacy-sha256-v1 (weak, fast, effectively unsalted SHA family) as deprecated: verify once, then RE-HASH to scrypt on first successful login (OWASP Password Storage Cheat Sheet — upgrade algorithm/work factor on login). The 1 null-hash row carries no secret and must be non-active. Crucially, these archival secrets are shielded by changes 1-3 (never in the api/readonly read surface; only authsvc reads them), so carrying them does not widen the request-path attack surface. RISK: the 2 legacy-sha256-v1 accounts stay verifiable until their owner next logs in — acceptable if upgrade-on-login is implemented and they are internal/low-value, otherwise force a reset for those 2; flipping the ghost to 'suspended' is safe (no password; its only value is the FK). Backfill runs as superuser so no privilege interaction — this is a pure value change.

fixes.research_basis: PostgreSQL 18 manual — 'Privileges' / GRANT (column-level SELECT/UPDATE, per-object grants vs. the indiscriminate ALL TABLES sweep that also targets views), ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES (standing defaults are owner-scoped and auto-apply to future objects), CREATE FUNCTION / 'Writing SECURITY DEFINER Functions Safely' (pin `SET search_path` to defeat the trojan search-path privilege-escalation, CVE-2007-2138 class), and Row Security Policies (BYPASSRLS/superuser bypass semantics — why the app pool must be neither). CIS PostgreSQL Benchmark — least-privilege application/service roles, REVOKE from PUBLIC, run the application as a dedicated non-superuser, and separate reporting from write roles. OWASP — Password Storage Cheat Sheet (never expose password hashes to the app/reporting surface; upgrade algorithm/work-factor on successful login for legacy schemes), Authorization/IDOR guidance (defense-in-depth so a single app-layer bug is not a total compromise), and Session Management (single, unpredictable session-identifier mapping → unique index on token_hash). Empirical grounding: every privilege claim reproduced on live umi_build_v3 and umi_backfill_v3 (PGPORT=5233) via has_table_privilege / has_column_privilege / has_schema_privilege / pg_default_acl / pg_indexes / pg_roles, plus the umi.effective_entitlement view's security_invoker=false (empty reloptions, owner juanlopez1→postgres) confirming the view can resolve entitlement without base-table grants.
fixes.risks: Scope discipline: all changes stay in 90_rls.sql / 00_foundation.sql / 30_runtime.sql / 99_verify.sql / backfill_identity.sql plus one backend guard in pg.service.ts — none touch the tenant RLS policies (lines 35-129 of 90_rls.sql) or the superuser-run backfill, so tenant isolation and lossless migration are preserved. Sequencing: the grant rewrite must run in 90_rls AFTER data lands (unchanged from 00_run_backfill.sh order), and the ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES REVOKE must execute as the same role that created the defaults (juanlopez1 locally, postgres on Supabase) or they persist. Behavioral breakage to expect and catch in the verify gate: `select *` from umi.user fails under column grants (enumerate columns); a dashboard billing screen reading umi.subscription/invoice as api must be re-routed (worker/authsvc/view); new tenant/runtime tables need an explicit grant and, for tenant, an explicit RLS policy once the api default is dropped. Biggest lift is change 1 (dedicated authsvc role + separate pool + SECURITY DEFINER session validation) — it is the defense-in-depth core and the only one that requires backend work; the revoke-only 'minimum tier' is necessary but insufficient while the worker/BYPASSRLS pool still performs auth writes. Supabase caveat: authsvc and the RLS-enforced api must be real LOGIN roles with their own rotated secrets, and BYPASSRLS on a custom role requires superuser (per vps-setup.md the worker path currently rides the `postgres` pooler role) — so 'ours to configure' is the grant model, role separation, search_path, indexes, and the boot guard; TLS/SCRAM/pg_hba remain Supabase-managed and are out of scope for this privileges pass.

fixes.summary: build-v3 gives the RLS-bound per-café request role `api` blanket SELECT on ALL of schema umi (incl umi.user.password_hash/password_algorithm, the whole cross-tenant RBAC/billing/pipeline/audit graph) and full INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on ALL of schema runtime (session, otp, password_reset_token, device_session, pairing, outbox_event, ...), while umi and runtime carry ZERO RLS (verified 0/16 each; tenant is 33/33). I reproduced, as scoped `api` with no app.current_business set: reading all 9 users + 8 password hashes, and INSERTing a forged runtime.session and a forged runtime.password_reset_token for an arbitrary user (rolled back). So a single request-path defect (IDOR/SQLi/dropped tenant predicate) = cross-tenant credential dump AND auth-token forgery / account takeover of Umi superadmin, with no DB layer beneath the app. Fix = least-privilege + defense-in-depth: strip api down to global catalogs + the owner-privileged entitlement view + non-secret user columns; move the password_hash read and all session/otp/reset issuance+consumption behind SECURITY DEFINER functions owned by a dedicated nologin `auth_owner` role (api gets EXECUTE, never table grants); make reset-token consumption a single atomic UPDATE ... WHERE used_at IS NULL RETURNING plus a UNIQUE index (kills the TOCTOU); strip credentials/tokens from `readonly`; and in the backfill DROP the archival password hashes (salt was left behind so they are both unverifiable AND uncrackable-as-carried, plus a 4-account shared password and 2 legacy-sha256 hashes) — NULL them and force status='invited' for all 9 users. Config-side: rotate the still-live Supabase/Twilio secrets and pin sslmode=verify-full on the VPS→Supabase link before cutover. Every change is gated by a has_*_privilege / index-existence / data assertion added to 99_verify.sql.

changes.what: Replace `api`'s blanket SELECT on schema umi with a catalog-only allowlist + the owner-privileged entitlement view + NON-SECRET columns of umi.user (no password_hash, no user_role/invoice/subscription/prospect/audit_log).
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql lines 24 and 27 (`grant select on all tables in schema umi to api;` and `alter default privileges in schema umi grant select on tables to api;`)
changes.sql_or_config:
-- DELETE line 24 and line 27. Replace with:
revoke select on all tables in schema umi from api;   -- idempotent: undo the blanket if re-run
grant select on
  umi.role, umi.permission, umi.role_permission,
  umi.channel_type, umi.feature, umi.plan, umi.plan_feature,
  umi.effective_entitlement
to api;
-- non-secret identity columns only (NO password_hash / password_algorithm):
grant select (id, email, full_name, status, last_login_at, created_at, updated_at)
  on umi.user to api;
-- NOTE: intentionally NO `alter default privileges in schema umi ... to api` — every
-- future umi table must be granted to api explicitly so a new sealed/credential table
-- is never auto-exposed to the café request role.
changes.rationale: Least privilege on the highest-blast-radius role. I verified umi.effective_entitlement has empty reloptions and owner=superuser, so it is NOT security_invoker — a view is read with its OWNER's privileges, so api keeps working entitlement gating through the view WITHOUT any grant on umi.subscription/plan_feature/entitlement_override. That lets us drop api's reach to billing (invoice/subscription/subscription_item), sales pipeline (prospect/prospect_event), the cross-tenant RBAC grant graph (user_role) and the sealed platform audit trail (audit_log). Removing the `alter default privileges` line closes the silent re-grant that today auto-hands api SELECT on any newly-created umi table.

changes.what: Introduce a dedicated nologin `auth_owner` role and expose the password_hash read + per-user grant resolution ONLY through SECURITY DEFINER functions (api gets EXECUTE, never a password_hash column grant). This removes the ability to bulk-dump credentials with one `select password_hash from umi.user`.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/00_foundation.sql (add auth_owner role next to api/worker/readonly); new file docs/migration/build-v3/40_auth_functions.sql run AFTER 30_runtime.sql and BEFORE 90_rls.sql; auth_owner's table grants live in 90_rls.sql
changes.rationale: scrypt is computed in Node (apps/umi-api/src/shared/auth/password.service.ts), so the hash must reach the app to verify a login — but it need not be reachable as a *table grant*. Encapsulating the read in a parameterized single-email SECURITY DEFINER function (Postgres-documented pattern for privileged reads) bounds an injection to one-hash-per-known-email instead of `select password_hash from umi.user` bulk exfiltration, and makes `select password_hash ...` as api fail outright. `set search_path=''` with fully-qualified names is the Postgres-mandated hardening for SECURITY DEFINER (defeats the CVE-2018-1058 search-path-hijack class). user_grants does the same for login-time authorization so api never holds SELECT on the cross-tenant umi.user_role RBAC map.
changes.verification: Gate: select has_function_privilege('api','umi.lookup_login_credential(text)','EXECUTE') and has_function_privilege('api','umi.user_grants(uuid)','EXECUTE') and (not has_column_privilege('api','umi.user','password_hash','SELECT')) and (select prosecdef and proconfig @> array['search_path='] from pg_proc where oid='umi.lookup_login_credential(text)'::regprocedure) and (select rolname='auth_owner' from pg_roles r join pg_proc p on p.proowner=r.oid where p.oid='umi.lookup_login_credential(text)'::regprocedure) as ok;

changes.what: Strip `api`'s full DML on all 16 runtime tables down to a least-privilege request-path allowlist, and remove ALL api access to the auth/token machinery (session, otp, password_reset_token, device_session, pairing). Session VALIDATION is exposed read-only via a definer function; session/token ISSUANCE stays off api entirely (worker/auth pool only), which is what actually blocks the forgery I reproduced.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql lines 26 and 29 (`grant select, insert, update, delete on all tables in schema runtime to api;` and the runtime `alter default privileges ... to api`); resolve_session in 40_auth_functions.sql; auth_owner runtime grants in 90_rls.sql
changes.sql_or_config:
-- DELETE line 26 and the runtime half of line 29. Replace with:
revoke select, insert, update, delete on all tables in schema runtime from api;  -- idempotent reset
-- api request-path allowlist ONLY:
grant select on runtime.product_embedding, runtime.message_embedding, runtime.knowledge_embedding to api;  -- RAG/search reads
grant select, insert on runtime.idempotency_key to api;   -- request idempotency
grant insert on runtime.inbound_event to api;             -- record inbound webhook (append-only)
grant insert on runtime.outbox_event to api;              -- enqueue outbound (append-only: no select/update/delete → cannot read or rewrite others' payloads or flip delivery status)
-- Everything else in runtime (session, otp, password_reset_token, device_session, pairing,
-- conversation_state, integration_sync, reminder_sent, pass_device, dead_letter): api gets NOTHING.
-- Reachable only via worker (BYPASSRLS) or the definer functions below.

-- read-only session validation for the request path:
grant select, insert, update, delete on runtime.session, runtime.otp, runtime.password_reset_token, runtime.device_session, runtime.pairing to auth_owner;
create function runtime.resolve_session(p_token_hash text)
  returns table (session_id uuid, user_id uuid, app text, expires_at timestamptz)
  language sql stable security definer set search_path = '' as $$
    select s.id, s.user_id, s.app, s.expires_at
      from runtime.session s
     where s.token_hash = p_token_hash
       and s.revoked_at is null and s.expires_at > now();
  $$;
alter function runtime.resolve_session(text) owner to auth_owner;
revoke all on function runtime.resolve_session(text) from public;
grant execute on function runtime.resolve_session(text) to api;
changes.rationale: runtime has no RLS and never will (sealed machinery), so ANY api table grant there is inherently cross-tenant/unfiltered — I minted a forged session and reset token as scoped api with no GUC. The only request-path auth operation is VALIDATING an incoming token; expose that as a read-only definer that returns a validated principal (never bulk token_hash), and keep issuance (mint session / issue+consume otp / issue+consume reset) on the worker or a dedicated auth pool that api cannot reach. resolve_session cannot forge (read-only) and cannot dump tokens. INSERT-only on outbox_event/inbound_event strips the SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE the finding flagged (reading cross-tenant payloads, flipping delivery status). Embeddings drop to SELECT (writes are the worker's job).
changes.verification: Gate: select bool_and(not has_table_privilege('api','runtime.'||t,'INSERT') and not has_table_privilege('api','runtime.'||t,'UPDATE') and not has_table_privilege('api','runtime.'||t,'DELETE') and not has_table_privilege('api','runtime.'||t,'SELECT')) from unnest(array['session','otp','password_reset_token','device_session','pairing']) t; -- must be TRUE. And: select has_function_privilege('api','runtime.resolve_session(text)','EXECUTE') and has_table_privilege('api','runtime.outbox_event','INSERT') and (not has_table_privilege('api','runtime.outbox_event','SELECT')) and (not has_table_privilege('api','runtime.session','SELECT')) as ok;

changes.what: Make password-reset consumption atomic (kills the TOCTOU replay) with a single UPDATE ... WHERE used_at IS NULL AND expires_at > now() RETURNING, exposed as a definer function, and back it with a UNIQUE index on token_hash.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/30_runtime.sql (add unique index on runtime.password_reset_token after the table, line ~53); consume function in 40_auth_functions.sql; app call site apps/umi-api/src/modules/auth/auth.service.ts:173-187 (replace the read-check-then-two-writes sequence)
changes.sql_or_config:
-- 30_runtime.sql (after the table def):
create unique index password_reset_token_hash_uq on runtime.password_reset_token (token_hash);

-- 40_auth_functions.sql:
create function runtime.consume_reset_token(p_token_hash text)
  returns uuid
  language sql volatile security definer set search_path = '' as $$
    update runtime.password_reset_token
       set used_at = now()
     where token_hash = p_token_hash
       and used_at is null
       and expires_at > now()
    returning user_id;
  $$;
alter function runtime.consume_reset_token(text) owner to auth_owner;
revoke all on function runtime.consume_reset_token(text) from public;
grant execute on function runtime.consume_reset_token(text) to auth_owner, worker;  -- NOT api
-- App (auth.service.resetPassword): const userId = await consume_reset_token(hash); if (!userId) throw 'invalid/expired/used'; then updatePassword(userId,...). One row wins under concurrency.
changes.rationale: OWASP Forgot-Password guidance requires reset tokens be single-use; today single-use is checked in JS then written in two separate non-transactional statements on the BYPASSRLS pool (confirmed auth.service.ts:173-187 / auth.repository.ts), so two concurrent replays both pass the used_at-null check. A single UPDATE that both filters on used_at IS NULL and sets it, RETURNING the row, is atomic under Postgres MVCC row locking — exactly one caller gets a row. The UNIQUE index on token_hash prevents duplicate/ambiguous tokens (the table today has only a pkey on id). Exposing it as a definer keeps consumption off the api role.
changes.verification: Gate: select (select count(*)=1 from pg_indexes where schemaname='runtime' and tablename='password_reset_token' and indexdef ilike '%unique%(token_hash)%') and has_function_privilege('worker','runtime.consume_reset_token(text)','EXECUTE') and (not has_function_privilege('api','runtime.consume_reset_token(text)','EXECUTE')) as ok;  -- plus a concurrency test: two parallel selects of consume_reset_token(h) on one live token return exactly one non-null user_id.

changes.what: Remove credential/token/financial exposure from the `readonly` (BI/diagnostics) role: it must never read password hashes, OTP/reset/session token hashes, or billing.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql lines 15-16 (`grant select on all tables in schema umi, tenant, runtime to readonly;` + the default-privileges line)
changes.rationale: readonly is the DSN most likely to be shared with BI tools / embedded in dashboards, and it has zero write need, so credential-leak likelihood is highest and write-blast-radius is nil. has_column_privilege(readonly,umi.user,password_hash,SELECT) and has_table_privilege(readonly,runtime.otp,SELECT) both return true today. Column-scoping umi.user (revoke table SELECT, then grant explicit non-secret columns) is the standard Postgres way to hide a sensitive column after a table grant. Audit/billing are sealed platform data with no diagnostic need in the default role.
changes.verification: Gate: select (not has_column_privilege('readonly','umi.user','password_hash','SELECT')) and (not has_table_privilege('readonly','runtime.otp','SELECT')) and (not has_column_privilege('readonly','runtime.session','token_hash','SELECT')) and (not has_table_privilege('readonly','runtime.password_reset_token','SELECT')) and (not has_table_privilege('readonly','umi.invoice','SELECT')) and (not has_table_privilege('readonly','umi.audit_log','SELECT')) and has_column_privilege('readonly','umi.user','email','SELECT') as ok;

changes.what: DATA: do NOT carry the archival password hashes into umi.user. NULL password_hash + password_algorithm for all 9 users and set status='invited' (preserving 'suspended'), forcing an invite/reset at cutover. This resolves the salt-loss lockout, the 4-account shared password, and the 2 legacy-sha256 hashes at once.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/backfill/backfill_identity.sql lines 31-41 (the `insert into umi.user ... select ... from core.users u`)
changes.sql_or_config:
insert into umi.user (id, email, password_hash, password_algorithm, full_name, status, created_at, updated_at)
select u.id,
       coalesce(nullif(u.email,''), 'migrated+'||u.id::text||'@umi.invalid'),
       null,                                   -- DROP archival hash: its per-user salt was left in source (umi.user has no salt column)
       null,                                   -- so scrypt is unverifiable (100% lockout) AND uncrackable-as-carried; also clears the shared/legacy liability
       coalesce(nullif(u.display_name,''), 'Unknown'),
       case when u.status in ('disabled','archived','suspended') then 'suspended'
            else 'invited' end,                -- every live human re-establishes a credential via invite/OTP at cutover (N=9, trivial)
       u.created_at, u.updated_at
from core.users u;
changes.rationale: Empirically: source core.users has password_salt for 8/8 hashed users but target umi.user has NO salt column, and password.service.verify(password, salt, hash) returns false without the salt → carrying the hashes silently locks out all 8 password users while providing zero login value. Because the salt is gone the 6 scrypt hashes are also uncrackable-as-carried, so they are pure credential-shaped liability; separately 4 accounts (admin+barista @ El Gran Ribera AND Kalala) share a byte-identical hash (one reused password across two tenants) and 2 are single-round legacy-sha256. NULLing + invited resolves availability (lockout), the reuse liability, and the legacy-hash liability in one move — the schema comment already defines status='invited' as 'no password yet'. Fixing the lockout by re-adding a salt column and backfilling the source salt would re-arm the shared-password/legacy crackability and is explicitly rejected.
changes.verification: Gate (run on umi_backfill_v3 after backfill): select (select count(*) from umi.user where password_hash is not null)=0 and (select count(*) from umi.user where password_algorithm is not null)=0 and (select count(*) from umi.user where status='active' and password_hash is null)=0 as ok;  -- i.e. no carried secrets, and nobody is 'active' with no credential.

A push_token alone is not directly abusable (sending a wallet push also needs Umi's server-side APNs/FCM key), so this is low severity — the value is minimization and consistency. Under changes #3 and #5 the token is already unreadable by api/readonly regardless.

changes.what: CONFIG (pre-cutover, non-SQL): rotate the still-pending Supabase JWT secret (invalidates the leaked service_role/anon keys) and the Twilio auth token, scrub/rewrite the leaked commits, pin the VPS→Supabase connection to verify-full TLS, and assert umi-api never connects as service_role. A live service_role key is BYPASSRLS and trumps every DB control above.
changes.where: prod config (Supabase project xbudk) + repo git history (rotation flagged pending in project memory) + umi-api VPS env (DATABASE_URL_*)
changes.sql_or_config:
# VPS env — pin certificate-verified TLS on every connection string:
DATABASE_URL_API=postgres://api@<xbudk-host>:5432/postgres?sslmode=verify-full&sslrootcert=/etc/ssl/supabase-ca.crt
DATABASE_URL_WORKER=postgres://worker@<xbudk-host>:5432/postgres?sslmode=verify-full&sslrootcert=/etc/ssl/supabase-ca.crt
# Pre-cutover checklist (ops):
#  1. Supabase → rotate JWT secret (kills leaked service_role + anon).  2. Rotate Twilio auth token.
#  3. git filter-repo / BFG to purge the leaked keys from history; force-push; invalidate caches.
#  4. Grep the umi-api config/DI: NO code path connects as service_role (request path = api, jobs = worker).
changes.rationale: sslmode=verify-full is the only libpq mode that authenticates the server certificate against a CA AND checks the hostname, preventing MITM/downgrade on the 'ours to configure' VPS→Supabase hop (CIS PostgreSQL Benchmark connection-security; libpq SSL docs; the weaker require/verify-ca do not stop impersonation). Supabase enforces TLS in transit as managed, so the local cluster's ssl=off is a red herring; the actionable item is the client pin. Rotating the JWT secret is the documented Supabase way to invalidate all issued service_role/anon keys; unrotated, a leaked BYPASSRLS service_role reads umi.user.password_hash and forges any runtime.session directly, bypassing the entire api/worker/RLS boundary this build hardens. Status is 'plausible' (rotation state not verifiable from this environment) but the cost of doing it is trivial and the impact if skipped is total.
changes.verification: Gate: on the running umi-api, `SELECT rolname, rolbypassrls FROM pg_roles WHERE rolname = current_user;` on both pools returns api(false) / worker(true) and NEVER service_role. And `SELECT ssl, version FROM pg_stat_ssl WHERE pid = pg_backend_pid();` returns ssl=t on the prod connection. CI/secret-scan (gitleaks/trufflehog) over full history returns 0 live Supabase/Twilio secrets.

changes.what: Add credential-boundary assertions to the build's self-check so every deploy/gate fails loudly if any of the above regresses (today 99_verify.sql checks schemas/roles/RLS-on-one-table but asserts NOTHING about api/readonly privileges on credentials).
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/99_verify.sql (extend the do-block)
changes.rationale: Defense-in-depth is only durable if it is asserted: privileges drift on every future GRANT/table-add. Turning each finding's has_*_privilege check into a build-time RAISE makes the credential boundary a gate (the project already runs a local verify gate before every push), so a re-introduced blanket grant or a new sealed table auto-exposed to api breaks the build instead of shipping silently.
changes.verification: Meta-gate: running the full build (00→99) against a pristine umi_build_v3 completes with 'build-v3 verify: OK' and no exception; deliberately re-adding `grant select on all tables in schema umi to api;` makes 99_verify.sql RAISE.

fixes.research_basis: PostgreSQL 18 manual: GRANT / privilege system and least-privilege (revoke blanket, grant per-object; ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES only where intended); CREATE VIEW privilege semantics — a non-security_invoker view executes with the OWNER's rights on base tables (verified empirically: umi.effective_entitlement has no reloptions, owner=superuser), the basis for keeping entitlement reads working after revoking base-table SELECT from api; SECURITY DEFINER hardening — the manual and the CVE-2018-1058 advisory mandate `SET search_path` (I use `search_path=''` + fully-qualified names) and REVOKE EXECUTE FROM PUBLIC on definer functions; Row Security Policies chapter (RLS as the intended cross-tenant guard — absent on umi/runtime, so grants are the only control there); MVCC/row-locking semantics of UPDATE ... WHERE ... RETURNING for atomic single-use token consumption; libpq SSL support (sslmode=verify-full is the only mode that verifies both CA chain and hostname); password_encryption=scram-sha-256 confirmed. CIS PostgreSQL Benchmark: least-privilege role design and avoiding broad grants to application roles; restricting superuser/BYPASSRLS to non-request paths; TLS-in-transit / certificate verification for client connections; secret rotation. OWASP: Forgot Password Cheat Sheet (reset tokens must be single-use, time-limited, high-entropy, stored hashed) — grounds the atomic consume + unique index; ASVS V2 (credential storage: never expose password hashes beyond the verifier; salted KDFs) and V3 (session management: server-side session integrity, no client-forgeable session issuance) — grounds moving hash reads + session issuance behind auth_owner; Secrets Management Cheat Sheet — grounds the Supabase/Twilio rotation + history scrub. All privilege/data claims reproduced live on umi_build_v3 and umi_backfill_v3 (PG 18.3): umi 0/16 & runtime 0/16 relrowsecurity vs tenant 33/33; api forged a runtime.session and password_reset_token with no app.current_business set; api reads 9 users/8 hashes; hash distribution scrypt x6 / legacy-sha256 x2 / null x1 with a 4-way byte-identical shared hash across El Gran Ribera + Kalala; source salt present 8/8 but absent in target; 398/398 pass_device push_tokens carried.
fixes.risks: ORDERING/IDEMPOTENCY: the umi and runtime blocks must REVOKE the blanket grant BEFORE the column/allowlist grants (a lingering table-level SELECT overrides column grants and would keep password_hash readable); every statement is written to be safe to re-run (revoke is idempotent, grants are additive). COORDINATED APP CHANGE REQUIRED (this is the big one): after these grants, umi-api MUST (a) read login credentials via umi.lookup_login_credential and authz via umi.user_grants instead of `select * from umi.user`/user_role; (b) authenticate incoming requests via runtime.resolve_session instead of a direct session SELECT; (c) call runtime.consume_reset_token; (d) run session/otp/reset ISSUANCE on the worker/auth pool, not the api pool. If the app still runs these as api after the grants land, login/reset/session break — so this must ship in lockstep with the app, which is why it is pre-cutover hardening, not a hot change. DEFINER OWNERSHIP: functions must be ALTERed to owner auth_owner (a nologin role holding only the specific table grants); if left owned by the superuser build role they still work but widen definer power — the locked search_path and fixed parameterized bodies (no dynamic SQL) mitigate, but the auth_owner ownership is the intended end state and is asserted in the gate. RESIDUAL CROSS-TENANT READ (adjacent, not credentials): umi.effective_entitlement returns all businesses; api reading it without `where business_id = current_setting('app.current_business',true)::uuid` is a cross-tenant entitlement read (not a credential leak) — recommend adding that predicate to the view or every call site (tenant-isolation area). OUTBOX RESIDUAL: api keeps INSERT-only on runtime.outbox_event so a request-path bug could still enqueue one message (delivery is worker-gated, payload is app-built, no cross-tenant read since SELECT is revoked); stronger option is a definer runtime.enqueue_outbox that stamps origin — deferred to avoid over-engineering. BACKFILL DATA: NULLing hashes means all 9 humans must (re)establish a credential via invite/OTP at cutover (N=9, an ops task, not silent); suspended/disabled accounts are preserved as 'suspended' so they are not re-invited. Dropping push_token means wallet pushes for the 398 passes pause until each device re-registers on its first post-cutover callback (brief continuity gap, same tradeoff already accepted for auth_token). READONLY: column-scoping umi.user breaks any BI query doing `select *` on it — reporting must name columns. BACKFILL RUNS AS SUPERUSER: none of these api/worker/readonly grants affect the backfill load (it runs as owner/superuser, BYPASSRLS), so the migration is unaffected — verified the backfill path is superuser. CONFIG ITEM is partly Supabase-managed: rotation state and git-history contents are not verifiable from this environment (hence 'plausible'), but the client-side sslmode=verify-full pin and the 'never connect as service_role' assertion ARE ours to enforce and cost nothing.

fixes.summary: The build-v3 DDL and backfill are structurally clean for SQL injection: the only dynamic SQL (60_triggers.sql, 90_rls.sql) uses execute format() with %I identifiers drawn from information_schema, never from data; no %s, no ||-concatenated executed SQL; all jsonb ->> extractions in the backfill flow into VALUES (data), never into SQL structure. Empirically verified in umi_build_v3: api has NO CREATE on public/umi/tenant/runtime (function-shadowing path closed today), tenant RLS is fail-closed (unset app.current_business -> NULL uuid -> 0 rows; empty/garbage -> hard "invalid input syntax for type uuid" error). The residual injection-class weakness is defense-in-depth, not a live exploit: (1) both shared trigger functions (public.tg_touch_updated_at, tenant.tg_append_only) run with proconfig=NULL — an unpinned search_path — which is safe under SECURITY INVOKER but is a latent CVE-2018-1058-style hijack if either is ever flipped to SECURITY DEFINER, and tg_append_only is the immutability guard on the two money ledgers; (2) the one out-of-DDL contract is that the umi-api backend must set app.current_business via a bound parameter (set_config(...,$1,true)), never by string-interpolating a request value into SET; (3) housekeeping — the backfill DB carries two legacy SECURITY DEFINER functions (public.user_has_business_access[_text], search_path=public, PUBLIC EXECUTE) inherited from the umi_prod_snapshot template that must be proven absent from the shipped clean build. All five fixes below are zero-to-low cost, do not touch tenant RLS semantics, and do not affect the backfill (which runs as superuser and only INSERTs into the append-only ledgers). Note (cross-cutting, owned by the grants/RLS area, not injection): the over-broad `grant select on all tables in schema umi to api` still exposes umi.user.password_hash — that grant is what turns any app-layer injection/IDOR into a credential breach; fixing it is out of this area's scope but is the multiplier that makes the search_path hardening worth doing.

changes.what: Pin a safe search_path on the shared updated_at trigger and fully-qualify its one catalog call, keeping it SECURITY INVOKER.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/00_foundation.sql:28-33 (public.tg_touch_updated_at)
changes.rationale: This function is attached by 60_triggers.sql to EVERY updated_at table and fires on every UPDATE under untrusted `api` sessions. Pinning search_path removes any dependence on the ambient/caller search_path for name resolution and neutralizes the search-path-shadowing class (CVE-2018-1058) proactively, so a future flip to SECURITY DEFINER or a future GRANT CREATE on public cannot silently reopen a hijack. `pg_catalog, pg_temp` (temp schema listed LAST) is the PostgreSQL-manual-recommended form for definer-safe functions; pg_catalog is searched first so now() resolves, and qualifying it as pg_catalog.now() makes it safe even if the SET is ever removed. Zero behavior change: the body only touches now() and the NEW record. Confirmed harmless to the backfill (touch fires on backfill UPDATEs; pg_catalog.now() resolves as superuser too).
changes.verification: select p.prosecdef = false and p.proconfig is not null and exists (select 1 from unnest(p.proconfig) c where c like 'search_path=%') as ok from pg_proc p join pg_namespace n on n.oid=p.pronamespace where n.nspname='public' and p.proname='tg_touch_updated_at';  -- must return ok = t

changes.what: Add a fail-loud pre-flight guard before the variant-price arithmetic cast so a malformed source price aborts with a descriptive, product-named error instead of an opaque mid-INSERT cast failure (and never silently drops a priced money row).
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/backfill/backfill_commerce.sql:66-84 (immediately before the '2b' insert into tenant.product_modifier that runs round((v->>'price')::numeric*100)::bigint at line 80)
changes.sql_or_config:
-- Pre-flight: every variant price must be numeric-coercible, else FAIL LOUD
-- (naming the product) rather than aborting the cutover on an opaque cast error.
do $$
declare bad text;
begin
  select string_agg(p.id::text || '=' || (v->>'price'), ', ')
    into bad
  from ops.products p
  cross join lateral jsonb_array_elements(p.variants) v
  where jsonb_typeof(p.variants) = 'array'
    and coalesce(btrim(v->>'name'),'') <> ''
    and (v->>'price') is not null
    and (v->>'price') !~ '^\s*\d+(\.\d+)?\s*$';
  if bad is not null then
    raise exception 'backfill_commerce: non-numeric variant price(s): %', bad;
  end if;
end $$;
changes.rationale: This is the one place source data flows through an arithmetic cast on a MONEY value. It is not an injection vector (the value never enters SQL structure — it is a scalar cast), but the info finding correctly flags it as an availability risk: a single non-numeric price would raise 'invalid input syntax for type numeric' and abort the whole cutover transaction with no indication of which product is at fault. Failing loud with the offending product id (rather than a WHERE-filter that would silently drop a priced variant and lose money data) is the right posture for a money migration. Source is currently clean (verified 1256 variant rows, 0 null prices), so this is a guard, not a repair.

changes.what: Codify the session-context contract in the 90_rls.sql header and add a standing fail-closed RLS gate proving app.current_business cannot leak tenant data when unset, blank, or non-uuid — the DB-side backstop for the app-layer requirement that the backend set the GUC via a bound parameter, never by string interpolation into SET.
changes.where: docs/migration/build-v3/90_rls.sql:1-6 (extend the header comment) + a new gate file docs/migration/build-v3/gate_rls_failclosed.sql wired into the per-PR RLS gate (run with psql -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1)
changes.rationale: The only injection residual that survives the static sweep is the app<->DB seam: if the backend built `SET app.current_business = '<request value>'` by concatenation, a crafted value could break out of the SET. Parameterizing via set_config($1) removes that entirely (OWASP SQL Injection Prevention: use bound parameters), and the existing `::uuid` cast in the policies is a second line of defense that rejects any non-uuid string with a hard error. The gate makes fail-closed a permanent, tested property rather than a one-off manual check; it directly re-runs the behavior confirmed empirically (unset -> 0 rows; empty '' -> invalid input syntax for type uuid). It changes no policy semantics and does not touch the backfill (superuser path).
